Just did some experiments on my Windows VM, and could reproduce this issue. Thanks for bringing this up! I just pushed a fix, which will be included in next release. For an instant fix, you can replace the content of file ``` C:\Program Files\Universal Radio Hacker\sdrplay_recv.py ``` with this:

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python2
##################################################
# GNU Radio Python Flow Graph
# Title: Top Block
# Generated: Fri Aug 21 15:56:13 2015
##################################################

from optparse import OptionParser

import tempfile
import os
import sys
try:
    with open(os.path.join(tempfile.gettempdir(), "gnuradio_path.txt"), "r") as f:
        gnuradio_path = f.read().strip()

    os.environ["PATH"] = os.path.join(gnuradio_path, "bin")
    # Append script path at end to prevent conflicts in case of frozen interpreter
    sys.path.append(sys.path.pop(0))
    sys.path.insert(0, os.path.join(gnuradio_path, "lib", "site-packages"))

except IOError:
    pass

from gnuradio import gr
from gnuradio.eng_option import eng_option
from grc_gnuradio import blks2 as grc_blks2
from InputHandlerThread import InputHandlerThread
import osmosdr
from gnuradio import zeromq

class top_block(gr.top_block):
    def __init__(self, samp_rate, freq, gain, bw, port):
        gr.top_block.__init__(self, "Top Block")

        ##################################################
        # Variables
        ##################################################
        self.samp_rate = samp_rate
        self.gain = gain
        self.freq = freq
        self.bw = bw

        ##################################################
        # Blocks
        ##################################################
        self.osmosdr_source_0 = osmosdr.source(args="numchan=" + str(1) + " " + "sdrplay")
        self.osmosdr_source_0.set_sample_rate(samp_rate)
        self.osmosdr_source_0.set_center_freq(freq, 0)
        self.osmosdr_source_0.set_freq_corr(0, 0)
        self.osmosdr_source_0.set_dc_offset_mode(0, 0)
        self.osmosdr_source_0.set_iq_balance_mode(0, 0)
        self.osmosdr_source_0.set_gain_mode(False, 0)
        self.osmosdr_source_0.set_gain(gain, 0)
        self.osmosdr_source_0.set_if_gain(gain, 0)
        self.osmosdr_source_0.set_bb_gain(gain, 0)
        self.osmosdr_source_0.set_antenna("", 0)
        self.osmosdr_source_0.set_bandwidth(bw, 0)

        self.zeromq_push_sink_0 = zeromq.push_sink(gr.sizeof_gr_complex, 1, 'tcp://127.0.0.1:' + str(port))

        ##################################################
        # Connections
        ##################################################
        self.connect((self.osmosdr_source_0, 0), (self.zeromq_push_sink_0, 0))

    def get_samp_rate(self):
        return self.samp_rate

    def set_samp_rate(self, samp_rate):
        self.samp_rate = samp_rate
        self.osmosdr_source_0.set_sample_rate(self.samp_rate)

    def get_gain(self):
        return self.gain

    def set_gain(self, gain):
        self.gain = gain
        self.osmosdr_source_0.set_gain(self.gain, 0)
        self.osmosdr_source_0.set_if_gain(self.gain, 0)
        self.osmosdr_source_0.set_bb_gain(self.gain, 0)

    def get_freq(self):
        return self.freq

    def set_freq(self, freq):
        self.freq = freq
        self.osmosdr_source_0.set_center_freq(self.freq, 0)

    def get_bw(self):
        return self.bw

    def set_bw(self, bw):
        self.bw = bw
        self.osmosdr_source_0.set_bandwidth(self.bw, 0)


if __name__ == '__main__':
    parser = OptionParser(option_class=eng_option, usage="%prog: [options]")
    parser.add_option("-s", "--samplerate", dest="samplerate", help="Sample Rate", default=100000)
    parser.add_option("-f", "--freq", dest="freq", help="Frequency", default=433000)
    parser.add_option("-g", "--gain", dest="gain", help="Gain", default=30)
    parser.add_option("-b", "--bandwidth", dest="bw", help="Bandwidth", default=200000)
    parser.add_option("-p", "--port", dest="port", help="Port", default=1337)
    (options, args) = parser.parse_args()
    tb = top_block(float(options.samplerate), float(options.freq), int(options.gain),
                   float(options.bw), int(options.port))
    iht = InputHandlerThread(tb)
    iht.start()
    tb.start()
    tb.wait()
```

@vasole I played around with this quite a bit over the past couple days, trying to track down in the C++ code where the problem was from.  It is definitely while reading the shell transition data.  In fact, by classic "print statement" debugging with the `labels` in `Shell::setRadiativeTransitions`, I saw some garbled looking values when it got to the 'L1Q3' label.  (And, really Q shell? Do those exist?).

Anyway, that inspired me to look at the data files in `fisx_data` that are being parsed.   Sure enough, L1Q3 is at the end of the line of `EADL97_LShellRadiativeRates.dat`.  And, sure enough, the files in the git repository have DOS line endings.  And,  sure enough, the code to parse these files as Spec files (really?) does not account for that.    Simply changing the line endings to UNIX line endings on these files means that the data can be read, and `Elements()` succeeds. 

Sorry, but this so very elegantly illustrates my point about using data tables in SQLite or other real database systems that I could not have possibly invented a better example.   XRF simulation code should not be parsing plain text files of fundamental constants at run-time. 

Anyway, at least now I understand how I can use fisx as a reference for testing other code.

The way I look at this, it is an infinite loop, so the fix only really has an effect on the first time round the loop -- it is an infinite cycle of "..  events, sleepers, ready ... ".

So the only effect of moving the code is that sleepers and ready stuff now get processed the first time round the loop, before the selector gets called, whereas in the past selectors were done first.

Now, because of the way curio works, the first time round the loop (on unix systems) an empty list of events will be returned, so the first iteration of the loop no events are processed anyway.

So the fix doesn't really change anything except avoiding calling selector_select when the selector is empty.

Plan B would be to do something like this at the top of the loop:

```python

   if selector._readers or selector._writers:
        events = selector_select(timeout)
  else:
        events = []

Note that this has the same (practical) result as moving the selector code to the end of the loop, since the first time round the loop the selector is always empty, so no event code gets executed anyway until the sleepers and ready stuff have been processed the first time round.

OK. Here is a first attempt at trying to make this happen: https://github.com/lkilcher/pint/commit/b4e2b4b74e23925c06f0c69f67b2ee6db0ee2f20

Note that according to the [docs](http://docs.scipy.org/doc/numpy-dev/reference/arrays.classes.html#special-attributes-and-methods), `__numpy_ufunc__` supercedes `__array_wrap__` + `__array_prepare__` when it is present. This appears to work as expected with py27 and a build of bleeding-edge numpy (git-clone).

I ran the unit tests, and this passes all but 5 of them. Two of those failures are due to the non-standard version number of the current numpy-dev branch.

The other three come-up in a `__ipow__` test. I haven't exactly figured out what is going on here, but I'm beginning to suspect that this may be an upstream issue in numpy. I created a minimal example of the bug here: https://github.com/lkilcher/pint/commit/abb0aaa7bff3c1d35c74279976c680e6ec190877. In particular, note that the test.py script fails with `TypeError: unsupported operand type(s) for ** or pow(): 'numpy.ndarray' and 'Quantity'`. This failure occurs for stable numpy (1.10.4), and a dev- build. However, if you don't define the `__numpy_ufunc__` method, the script runs fine.  Also note that this issue only seems to arise for `**=`. Other operators/statements (`**`, `*=`, etc.) seem to work fine. This may have something to do with the second note under the `__numpy_ufunc__` [docs](http://docs.scipy.org/doc/numpy-dev/reference/arrays.classes.html#special-attributes-and-methods), but I haven't exactly figured out why it's only an issue for `**=`, and not `*=` or others.

Also, at this point I've mostly just copied code from your `__array_wrap__` method. Assuming that will be left in-place for backward compatability, it may be worthwhile to move the duplicate code to other sub-methods. I didn't do anything like this for this demonstration code, and because I couldn't quite decipher what you are doing with the long `try: ... except:` block in `__array_wrap__`.

Anyway, let me know what you think.

Oh no, even `Call(tuple, args=[T['id'], T])` because that will do `tuple(T['id'], T)` -- what should happen is `tuple([T['id'], T])` -- although if you had a clean way to construct a list of two elements, you could skip the `Call` because list-of-lists-of-two-elements is fine itself as an argument to the dict constructor...


Hmm.....

So, `Check` still seems like the most direct way to solve this problem and was already on the roadmap / partly written (https://github.com/mahmoud/glom/blob/check/glom/core.py#L399-L447) so I'll proceed with that.  Semantics for these kind of explicit lists might also be in the near future though.

Some thoughts -- 

`Record([T['id'], T], type=tuple)` -- record is just very explicit about what is intended

`[T['id'], T]` -- this reads nicely, but feels awkward -- list of 1 item means "unpack the target", list of 2 items means "join the results of multiple together"; seems error prone

Maybe another way to do this would be with zip

```python
glom(target, (Call(zip, args=[[T['id']], T]), dict, T[0]) )
```

def wsearch(location):
    resultlist = []
    search = owm.weather_at_places(location,searchtype='accurate')

        country = l.get_country()

            lat = str(l.get_lat())

            resp = data.json()
            county = resp['result'][0]['admin_county']
            if county == None:

            country = resp['result'][0]['country']
            names = name+"-"+county+"-"+country
            resultlist.append(ID+":"+names)

@MinchinWeb you're welcome!

Windows is known to be problematic in Python (e.g. most of packaging are about windows and binaries).

Python is a case sensitive language, your directory/file names become package/module names, this is how it works [PEP-0235, look at `Proposed Semantics`, case `B`](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0235/): 
```
search sys.path for the first case-sensitive match; raise
       ImportError if none found
```

Moreover, the consensus [coding style](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0008/#package-and-module-names) says
> Modules should have short, all-lowercase names. Underscores can be used in the module name if it improves readability. Python packages should also have short, all-lowercase names, although the use of underscores is discouraged.

That is some impressive work!  Having worked on green so much myself, I can guess how much time and effort you put into this.

I really appreciate you bringing this every-test-is-loading-and-running-class-and-module-setup-and-teardown issue to my consciousness.  I should have been aware of this fact, but as my day-job organization (the primary place where I use this project) doesn't use module or class setup/teardown, this issue is present much more as a side effect of the other design decisions, rather than as a conscious choice.

I read through all your code, and I spent a few hours pondering about what would be the best thing to do.  There are several viable options that I came up with (one of which would be polishing up your code), and in the end I hope you like the conclusion I came to.

I'm going to lead you through my reasoning first:

1) I think I went down a non-optimal path in the first place with the fundamental design where each dotted test name is passed individually to subprocesses and each test is loaded completely independently.  I did consider the effect of a module potentially being loaded in each subprocesses, and each test creating an independent copy of the entire TestCase object.  At the time I judged that as an acceptable trade-off for a fairly straight-forward loading design (which has become less and less straight-forward as time has gone on), since importing modules and creating TestCase objects tend to be pretty minor time-wise in comparison to the actual tests themselves

However, I failed to even consider the module and class setup/teardown cases.  As you pointed out, duplicating that logic, which it can be reasonably assumed is done at that level _because_ it is time consuming, is going to tend to impose an unacceptable performance cost

2) Green's code regarding multiprocess functionality is more complicated than I would like it to be (_before_ this pull request), which I think has prevented several potential contributors from really digging in and making their own pull requests.

After your pull request, quite frankly I don't think even I would want to touch green's multiprocess stuff any more.  It's getting really complicated to follow.  Really hard to fit the design in your head all at once.  [It may not seem too bad to you now, but wait until you come back to it after a year of not touching it much - trust me. :-) ]

3) I would really like green to run in multiple processes by default.  Why doesn't it?  Because a lot of existing tests that were written without running them through green are not multi-process safe.  They assume most often that the tests in a TestCase class are run in a specific serial sequence.  Less often they assume that the TestCases in a module are run in a specific serial sequence.  However, I haven't often (maybe ever?) noticed any assumptions that multiple _modules_ would be run in a specific serial sequence...

---

Which brings me to my conclusion.  After having considered several different options, I think the best course of action is to **scrap the current design** which transmits-dotted-test-names one by one to subprocesses which then load them up as if each were completely independent.  Then replace that with **a design that primarily transmits dotted names of entire modules, which are then loaded up and run "normally" within a given subprocess.**

PROS

* No special code needed to handle module setup/teardown
* No special code needed to handle class setup/teardown
* Assuming a relatively high number of modules, overall test runs can be much faster due to much less duplicate importing and setup/teardown
* We could end up with less code and a simpler design than we have right now, which is both easier to maintain and easier for others to start contributing to.
* We should be able to have green automatically pick the number of processes to run by default (instead of just using 1) while still having a high degree of confidence that most existing test suites will not break (or will require minimal modifications to make concurrent-safe).  This alone should make most non-customized green runs faster by default (assuming more than one module is present).
* I'm willing to pitch in and give this a try

CONS

* You can only get performance enhancements from concurrency at the module level.  Only have 1 module?  Then no speedup is possible, by design.  (Though you still get the other benefits of running the tests through green)
* The existing design of green's process handling will need to be significantly refactored.  That's still real work.
* Output may not be as smooth as it is currently.  Instead of individual unit tests being run in subprocesses, and then output coming out in order fairly smoothly (only individual long tests causing output hiccups), instead you will get a single process's test results at 1-processor speed, and then when it is done you may have a huge chunk of output ready from other subprocesses that will then blink past very quickly.

That's my take on things, anyway.  What are your thoughts?

----

On a completely separate, but very related note, I have [a branch where I am polishing per-subprocess setup/teardown](https://github.com/CleanCut/green/tree/process-overhaul).  I have held off merging it and releasing it, because I have been making lots of backward-incompatible option and behavior changes.

That is where I would likely also start acting on my proposal above.  If you are interested, I invite you to branch off of that branch instead of master.

Yep, this is a known issue and basically is a result of a compromise I made during performance optimization.

Discussed a little bit at https://github.com/daler/gffutils/issues/20#issuecomment-17973528 and summarized at http://pythonhosted.org/gffutils/developers.html#little-things. Quoting the latter.

> * I really wanted to maintain round-trip invariance: importing into a db and then getting the features back out should not change them at all. That’s where the dialect comes into play – it specifies the format of the attributes string, which is the trickiest thing to get right.
* at first, I was keeping track of the order of attributes in an OrderedDict. Benchmarking with 1M+ line files showed that this was slow. So now the attributes are stored as plain ol’ dicts, and information about the order of attributes is stored only once: in the db’s dialect. While features with different orders of attributes (on one line “gene_id” comes first; on another line “Name” comes first) will be round-trip invariant, this should at least hold for most cases. I figured it was a good compromise.

I added more information to the `gffutils.create_db` docstring to help clarify this.

No, really, the DLIB_ASSERT statement in find_min_single_variable() is only enabled when compiled in debug mode.  Specifically, this only happens when ENABLE_ASSERTS, DEBUG, or _DEBUG is #defined.  So if you compile in visual studio in Release or RelWithDebInfo then the DLIB_ASSERT will be off and no fatal_error will thrown and assert() and abort() will never be called.  This is true no matter how you call find_min_single_variable().  

This means you shouldn't rely on fatal_error being thrown to provide you with input validation from your user.  You must do as you show in this code snippet, explicitly validate your user inputs yourself however you think is appropriate.

If you fail to call a dlib function correctly, dlib will inform you via the mechanism you are complaining about.  But only if compiled in debug mode, which it sounds like you aren't doing.  So there is nothing to worry about, assert() will never be called.  You also seem to be suggesting that you would compile dlib's code in debug mode for a deployed application.  That is a very bad idea as it will be extremely slow due to all the DLIB_ASSERT statements.  

Or are you saying that, while you were debugging your application, you had some difficulty where, during a single run inside the debugger, 2 DLIB_ASSERTS failed and it was somehow difficult to figure out how to fix the bug?

```python
In [1]: import numpy as np

   ...:     [0.0, 0.0, 0.0],

   ...: ])

```python
In [8]: import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

In [14]: ax.plot(points[:, 0], points[:, 1], points[:, 2])

I agree completely with the first point (I'll commit a change).

For the second point, I just didn't want to mess with anything that was working. There is one issue that I can see for implementing long strings. Currently we don't support reading or writing continue statements (they are treated like comment cards and are not updated if the header card value is changed). If we implemented the long string write, then we would be allowing people to write long strings, but not read them back in. This would probably lead to some confusion, and it is probably better to just raises an exception for long string values until long string support is fully implemented.

I am interested in resolving this issue for the windows platform and may contribute if I have time. The main information that I would like to clarify before beginning is what the general architecture for this will be. Personally I believe the best option in a practical sense would be to allow the user to register key combinations to be blocked (this could be done by simply adding suppress=True to any applicable functions). If done this way, suppression can be done only purely within keyboard, which would allow delays to be strictly controlled. 

Moving to the delays specifically: suppression is something that may need to be done using cython. What I'm envisioning is something like this:

1. User calls `keyboard.add_hotkey(...suppress=True)`
2. Everything that currently happens now happens. But in addition, keyboard stores they key combination in `keys_to_suppress`.
3. Keyboard registers an os-specific cython module with the operating system that simply compares input to the `keys_to_suppress` and suppresses the input if there is a match.
4. Keyboard calls the user function, which may or may not duplicate (pass through) the original request.

Regarding your third point, that would simply be up to the user. So if the user added `suppress=False` (which would be the default) to the ctrl+a, space, then input would not be blocked. But if the user added `suppress=True`, then it would. Input would only be blocked up until the point where it would not be able to satisfy `keys_to_suppress`.

As for where the methods come from, I reckon those are inherited because of the `extra` keyword in the definition of [`typing.MutableSet`](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/3.6/Lib/typing.py#L1832) which refers to [`collections.abc.MutableSet`](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/5bf74859fe253f0e8ff73ba699cd1a7e2dfbcea3/Lib/_collections_abc.py#L556), where `pop()` and friends are defined. But [`typing.Set`](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/3.6/Lib/typing.py#L1895) also inherits from the builtin `set`, so I don't know where it ends up looking. It's all a bit magic because of the liberal use of metaclasses :-)

Thanks for the PR! I have a few thoughts here:

(1) We should definitely add a timeout to the `_wake_w` socket! But I'm not sure that we should inherit the `max_block_ms`. I would prefer that we use a different name in the client, like `wakeup_block_ms`, and have the KafkaProducer pass that config to KafkaClient (probably still based on max_block_ms). This will help if/when we need to also support in the KafkaConsumer. I also think the default at the client level can and should be much lower than 60000.

(2) Are you expecting the KafkaProducer to raise an exception if there is a connection error? That is not our current design and I am hesitant to add that without a lot of thought. I would prefer to log a warning, the same as we do on `socket.error`

(3) But so in normal operation the wake socket pair should be drained very frequently and we should not see blocked writes. So the fact that this happens at all is strange. It suggests that the background producer thread is blocked (or crashed) somewhere as well. I would love to figure out where that is and fix it, because that seems like the root cause. In your local testing can you print `sys._current_frames()` when you detect a blocked socket before raising the exception and post the results?

While it's certainly more pythonic, I'm not too excited about ducktyping this:

1. Dates/times are complex enough that I expect there's a number of semi-valid inputs where the conversion won't blow up, but also won't coerce the value to what the user expects, resulting in silently corrupted timestamps. For example, what if someone thinks `timestamp_ms=True` causes the producer to append the current timestamp?
2. A common use case for message timestamps in Kafka is to support message re-processing when you realized the last deploy pushed a bug in your code. Just fix the bug and re-process all messages since the last deploy. However, if you aren't watching consumer lag based on timestamps, you may not realize that your timestamps are being coerced incorrectly until you're in the middle of firefighting. So it feels like database backups... if there's a chance of failure, I'd rather blow up early and let the human decide what to do.
3. Are there any negative performance impacts for the broker's time-based index if multiple producers are sending to a topic, and one of them is sending incorrect timestamps? For example, the `timestamp_ms=True` which coerces to `1`, does that mean the broker is constantly switching between appending the valid entries to the end of the index and then going back to the beginning for the erroneous entries? Perhaps this is fine, I'm not very familiar with the time-based offsets functionality/implementation, as we are still ~6 months away from turning them on at my day job

hah - snark! unfortunately I won't merge this. I'm willing to consider other approaches, but using the default request_timeout_ms is not ok (indeed, that is why there's a timeout parameter internally). This would mean that by default the check_version in consumer and producer instantiation could block up to 40secs! Unless you can prove that there is no broker compatibility case that will cause a timeout (I am not convinced of this), then I think the timeout needs to be small here.

My suggestion remains -- if you are concerned about network latency, do not depend on dynamic broker version checks. The approach I've taken here is very controversial and is not explicitly supported by the kafka server development team. It is, in other words, a HACK.

A "message" in kafka is encoded as an (Int64 offset, Bytes payload). The v0 payload is encoded as (Int32 crc, Int8 magic, Int8 attributes_flags, Bytes key, Bytes value). v1 payload adds an Int64 timestamp. There is also a newer v2 format, but it is not used by 0.10 brokers.

In your case we are able to decode the offset but the payload is empty -- it is a 0-length byte field. As far as I know that is not supposed to ever happen in data coming back from a kafka broker. Every payload should decode into the full field list above w/ keys and values. So we could improve the error message here, but it does seem like this is a bug in the data returned by the kafka 0.10 broker.

kafka-python should be resilient to network failures, so I dont think the error message suggests message loss.

You might consider enabling a more verbose logging level to get deeper insight into the conditions causing the error.

It was my impression that you can execute functions in an ELF file by memory mapping it, finding the bytecode for the function as an offset relative to the base pointer of the memory map, casting that as a function pointer, and then calling the function. In my case, the ELF bytes are already in memory and shouldn't have to touch disk. The hard part, whether the file comes from disk or starts in memory, is finding the function's bytecode within the ELF. I'm hoping that pyelftools can help with that by interpreting the headers for me.

Actually saving my ELF string as a file and loading it with dlopen/dlsym (or ctypes) would be an unsatisfactory but usable solution if it worked, but it doesn't work. You weren't surprised that it didn't work, that there are object files that can't be linked/reloaded.

I know that the information needed to reconstruct the executable function is in this particular ELF file, since it's possible to transport it and reconstruct it with the full Numba/llvmlite/LLVM toolchain: https://stackoverflow.com/a/42198101/1623645

But in principle all of those dependencies shouldn't be required on the remote machines (which don't actually compile anything), since ELF is a widely recognized standard, and the remote machines have the same instruction set as the compiler. I was able to find a workaround for at least my version of this problem:

- If the table doesn't exist
  - create a temp table with datetimes as string
  - insert data as string
  - create actual table by selecting * from this temp table and cast datetime columns as the correct format
  - drop the temp table
- Else if the table exists already
  - insert datetime fields as string using the same `cursor.executemany()` statement above

Once the table is created and populated with values, inserting string fields in the equivalent format as the DB expects into DATETIME fields seems to work without any errors. This works for Teradata flavor of SQL at least, but have not tried out other flavors.

We could keep the integration tests separate by having the integration tests in a separate test folder which is explicitly excluded from the default test suite. There are two classes of integration tests that I see.

I too am playing with filters and your program.  I notice that in the extract_audio function the audio is down mixed to mono.  I found that by applying the filter to help eliminate phase errors before the down mix, the resulting audio was higher quality and resulted in more text being converted from the file.
See: https://trac.ffmpeg.org/wiki/AudioChannelManipulation

A small problem with this filter, is sometimes (for some reason i can't work out) that filter causes ffmpeg to crash, in which case I just fall back to a straight conversion to mono with no phase correction.

Also, filtering ambient noise using the tips from: https://manerosss.wordpress.com/2017/07/24/ffmpeg-%C2%B7-apply-a-filter-to-enhance-voice-by-removing-low-and-high-frequency-noises/

Improved word recognition.  More words in my samples were recognised, and the translations made more sense, generally.

Finally I then used the tool: ffmpeg-normalize to do an automatic normalisation, as in Method 3 above.

Using all three methods in this order resulted in a significant increase in detected words, and translation accuracy.

Sorry, but I may be failing to understand the question. In OOP it's customary to call the base class overriden method.

``SMA`` is an alias for ``MovingAverageSimple`` and its ``__init__`` method is being called, which in turns uses ``super`` (to enable cooperative inheritance if needed be) to call its base class ``__init__`` method.

```python
class MovingAverageSimple(MovingAverageBase):
    '''
    Non-weighted average of the last n periods

    Formula:
      - movav = Sum(data, period) / period

    See also:
      - http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Moving_average#Simple_moving_average
    '''
    alias = ('SMA', 'SimpleMovingAverage',)
    lines = ('sma',)

    def __init__(self):
        # Before super to ensure mixins (right-hand side in subclassing)
        # can see the assignment operation and operate on the line
        self.lines[0] = Average(self.data, period=self.p.period)

        super(MovingAverageSimple, self).__init__()
```

That's a possible approach. But it has an inherent problem: the initial value and the minimum period.

You look back with `[-1]` and that means `next` cannot be called until at least one other value has been put  there.

And you need to put some initial value there, because if not, the system fills the values automatically with `NaN` and `NaN + 1 = NaN`

Your best bets:

  - Subclass from `backtrader.indicators.PeriodN` which already carries a `period` parameter and knows how to pass this information to the system.

    Change the value of the parameter `period` (default is `1`) to at least `2`. Or larger if you need a larger warm-up for your function.

  - Fill the array with default values (probably `0`) in the `prenext` method

There is nothing against optimizing a list of floats. backtrader's code doesn't care if a list contains int, float or objects. It must simply be an iterable.

```python
class add_qr_functor:
    import pyqrcode
    def __init__(self):
        import StringIO
        import re
        self.buffer = StringIO.StringIO()
        self.pattern = re.compile('<svg.*>(<path.*>)</svg>')

    def __call__(self, x, y, text, scale=1):
        qr = self.pyqrcode.create(text)
        self.buffer.truncate(0)
        qr.svg(self.buffer, xmldecl=False, quiet_zone=0, scale=scale)
        match = self.pattern.match(self.buffer.getvalue())
        result_list = [match.group(1)]
        if x != 0 or y != 0:
            result_list.insert(0, '<g transform="translate(%s,%s)">' % (x, y))
            result_list.append('</g>')
        return '\n'.join(result_list)

add_qr = add_qr_functor()
```

Requiring the user to wrap the callbacks in `try/except` blocks and printing the traceback to stdout or somehow pass it to a place where it is *not* caught by the `try/except` block in the `pynput` library without an option to turn it off is, in my opinion, a weird design choice. Hmm, I don't have anything as old as 2.6.1 :-). 2.7.1 exports 193 symbols, from:

```
nm -D /usr/lib64/libfreetype.so.6 | grep ' T ' | wc -l
```

whille mingw freetype 2.6.5 exports 190:
```
winedump -j export /usr/i686-w64-mingw32/sys-root/mingw/bin/libfreetype-6.dll
winedump -j export /usr/x86_64-w64-mingw32/sys-root/mingw/bin/libfreetype-6.dll
```

freetype uses gcc's symbol visibility functionality to hide internal routines, so what's exported is meant to be public and therefore these are fairly authoritative figure. AFAIC, freetype-py (from `freetype-py/freetype/raw.py` ) uses 65 currently, so there is a long way to go.

By comparison, SharpFont (C# binding for freetype) gets at 191.

```
SharpFont]$ grep DllImport Source/SharpFont/FT.Internal.cs  | wc -l
191
```

So SharpFont is more or less complete to current - although, from personal first-hand experience, much of it not tested or even been used. Also as C# is a bit further from the C-API than freetype-py, when it is not working correctly, not as obvious as freetype-py. 

BTW, freetype 2.7.2 or 2.8.0 (the next one after 2.7.1) is coming out soon in a metter of days.

550d078<https://eur01.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fgithub.com%2Flwanger%2Fcooked_input%2Fcommit%2F550d0788301d0e0fbdcf9beab010b074decc2254&data=02%7C01%7C%7C1bbce9d1f4dd43a7cb4508d5bf16cf32%7C84df9e7fe9f640afb435aaaaaaaaaaaa%7C1%7C0%7C636625027035873107&sdata=EfEPttb4mAD9ELOwFiA%2FGGr6MXPTMXOR%2Fzu6rVKvoTA%3D&reserved=0> fixes this issue by specifying the necessary requirements in setup.py. Branched of the V0.2.12 tag.

While doing that, I realized that I can't even pip install the package due to some missing modules.
I found out that the module itself is imported in setup.py, which in turn triggers all the imports specified in the __init__.py file, which holds imports to packages that are not installed by default (e.g. prettytable).

Since this import is only used to retrieve the version string, I fixed that in 0f8f52d<https://eur01.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fgithub.com%2Flwanger%2Fcooked_input%2Fcommit%2F0f8f52d8aa8d36779d0bc7d4f837e6ae99b6cefa&data=02%7C01%7C%7C1bbce9d1f4dd43a7cb4508d5bf16cf32%7C84df9e7fe9f640afb435aaaaaaaaaaaa%7C1%7C0%7C636625027035873107&sdata=4GdDJ6%2Bl%2BwpVYZrJG1jNLsQHGkJYM%2B0lGH9joQxA%2BNA%3D&reserved=0> to read it from a single version.py file instead.

One more thing: I did not touch the wrong (?) package name cooked-input in the setup.py file yet, although I think that should also be changed...

OK, I figured it out.  Even `pandoc demo.md -t json | python ../pandoc_eqnos.py | pandoc -f json -o demo.pdf` worked for me, but using an installed a setuptools entry-point would not.  The reason is that *STDOUT (a TextIOWrapper on Python3) may or may not be flushed at program exit* (http://bugs.python.org/issue17852), and it apparently wasn't when the entry-point was used.

Adding a call to `STDOUT.flush()` at the end of `main` solved the issue.

* Add a option in the `Sequence` constructor to disable wrapping of subsequences. This way at the very least there is a way to avoid it entirely.

Hmm, there's a slight problem with using `radialrange()` in `Line.point_to_t()`: performance.

The original "by-hand" implementation takes ~75 microseconds per call in my test (on my hardware), the updated implementation that calls `radialrange()` takes ~315 microseconds.  I guess this is not surprising since `radialrange()` answers a much more general, more complicated question than what `point_to_t()` really needs.

Here's my test script:
~~~~~
#!/usr/bin/env python2

import svgpathtools
import random
import resource

num_points = 30000
l = svgpathtools.path.Line(start=(0+0j), end=(10+10j))

random.seed(0)
test_points = [complex(random.uniform(-5, 15), random.uniform(-5, 15)) for i in range(num_points)]

start = resource.getrusage(resource.RUSAGE_SELF)
for p in test_points:
    l.point_to_t(p)
end = resource.getrusage(resource.RUSAGE_SELF)

total = (end.ru_utime + end.ru_stime) - (start.ru_utime + start.ru_stime)
print("%.6f seconds/point" % (total/num_points))
~~~~~

That's... a really interesting question.

So I think the discrepancy here is that sRGB actually specifies two different luminance conditions: notice that in that table on wikipedia, there's the "viewing environment" luminance at `80 cd/m^2`, and the "encoding ambient illuminance level" at `64 lux = 64/pi cd/m^2 ~= 20.4 cd/m^2`.

It's not obvious which of these numbers CIECAM02 should be using. Currently, colorspacious uses the latter. This is independent of the "serious bug", which is that we were calculating `L_A` as `20.4 cd/m^2 * 5 ~= 100` instead of `20.4 cd/m^2 / 5 ~= 4`. So that was definitely a bug that needed fixing independently of this issue :-).

However, I really don't understand the games that the sRGB standard is playing with "viewing" versus "encoding" conditions, and it could be that the you're right, and the `80 cd/m^2` value is the one that we ought to be using. The reason I did it this way is that [Moroney (2002)](http://citeseerx.ist.psu.edu/viewdoc/download?doi=10.1.1.108.1199&rep=rep1&type=pdf), when giving recommendations for how to use CIECAM97s, the precursor to CIECAM02, says:

"For example, the sRGB standard has an ambient illumination level of 64 lux or an `L_A` value of 4. Annex D in the sRGB standard provides a typical office ambient illumination of 350 lux or an `L_A` value of 22. [...]

"An example is useful to illustrate how surround is determined. For the sRGB specification, the ambient illumination level is 64 lux and the monitor white is 80 cd/m^2. Using equation 1 the surround luminance can be computed to be 20.4 cd/m^2..."

From this I think (but am not certain!) that what's going on is the sRGB spec assumes that we are looking at a monitor that outputs 80 cd/m^2, in a room whose lighting is otherwise 64 lux, and that it's the latter number we're supposed to use for computing `L_A`.

If this is wrong, then I'd very much like to know, though I'm not sure who to ask...

Note also that the parameters from the sRGB spec may or may not be a good fit for your particular setting. The 64 lux value definitely corresponds to a rather dim setting (I think it's supposed to simulate, like, watching a movie with the lights off and curtains closed), and if you'd rather do calculations assuming a "typical office environment" then you're free to use a different `L_A` regardless of what sRGB says. But I do want to know what sRGB says, because I think that's the only reasonable way for us to set our defaults, even if it turns out to be a bit weird.

But it seems to work.

```
from tkinter import *
import pandas as pd
from pandastable import Table, TableModel
from threading import Thread
import time

class TestApp(Frame):
        """Basic test frame for the table"""
        def __init__(self, parent=None):
            self.parent = parent
            Frame.__init__(self)
            self.main = self.master
            self.main.geometry('800x600+200+100')
            self.main.title('Table app')
            f = Frame(self.main)
            f.pack(fill=BOTH,expand=1)
            df = TableModel.getSampleData(rows=5)
            self.table = pt = Table(f, dataframe=df, showtoolbar=True )
            pt.show()
            self.startbutton = Button(self.main,text='START',command=self.start)
            self.startbutton.pack(side=TOP,fill=X)
            self.stopbutton = Button(self.main,text='STOP',command=self.stop)
            self.stopbutton.pack(side=TOP,fill=X)
            self.table.showPlotViewer()            
            return

        def update(self):
            """Iterate over some fake data and update table and plot"""
            
            table=self.table              
            plotter = table.pf
            opts = plotter.mplopts
            plotter.setOption('linewidth',3)
            plotter.setOption('kind','line')
            #opts.widgets['linewidth'].set(3)
            #opts.widgets['kind'].set('line')
            data = TableModel.getSampleData(rows=10000)
            data = data.set_index('date')
            w=20
            for i in range(0,len(data),w):
                new = data[i:i+200]
                #new = new.rolling(w).mean()
                table.model.df = new               
                table.multiplecollist=range(0,6)
                table.redraw()
                table.plotSelected()
                time.sleep(.1)
                if self.stop == True:
                    return
            return

        def start(self):
            self.stop=False
            t = Thread(target=self.update)
            t.start()            

        def stop(self):
            self.stop = True
            return

app = TestApp()
#launch the app
app.mainloop()
```

Yes. There is a scanning optimization in place that will favour string match with greater length.
You can see the logic [here](https://github.com/igordejanovic/parglare/blob/master/parglare/tables.py#L267).
`finish_flags` are used as a "cutoff" during scanning. This logic will order string matches at the beginning, from longest to shortest, after that it will try regexes. If string match succeeds it will try no other as this was the most specific match. This, again, is optimization for which there could be a flag for disabling in the future versions if needed. In computer language development, this is what is needed almost always, but I understand that in NLP it might not be.

It just feels funny to try to reproduce the work that `pkgconfig` developers already went to by implementing our own copy here and potentially incurring maintenance burden by doing so.

This is why I was thinking this could be optional (i.e. `try...except ImportError`). That feels appropriate IMHO as this is a bit of a special case. So we would just expect users to have `pkgconfig` if they want to do this, but not bother if they don't.

If we want to ensure `pkgconfig` is always present and do not want to go the `setuptools`/`setup_requires` route, an alternative would be to include a vendorized `pkgconfig` simply for building purposes. We can even excluded it from being installed.

Perhaps a third option is just to add flags to `setup.py` that allow one to explicitly set the library and include paths to use for linking to LZMA. This avoids having a special relationship with `pkg-config` and allows a user to set these whatever way they wish.

Thanks for reporting this. It's a bug (I guess?) with ffmpeg itself, not
with my script. For some reason, the EBU loudness filter changes the sample
rate of the audio stream, which makes it less compatible with some players
that apparently can't handle 96 kHz sample rate. This has also been asked
here <https://superuser.com/q/1218471/48078>.

You can circumvent this by adding an extra option to downsample to 48 kHz
again:

ffmpeg-normalize -u -v -b -a aac -e "-b:a 96k -ar 48000"

I've reported this as a bug to FFmpeg.

Hi @jpoa ,
`pytablewriter` will automatically determine alignment for each of the columns from the data type.
Integer or real-numbers are left aligned, other data types will right aligned.

For example:

```py
df = pd.read_csv(six.StringIO(dedent("""\
    "i","f","c","if","ifc","bool","inf","nan","mix_num","time"
    1,1.10,"aa",1.0,"1",True,Infinity,NaN,1,"2017-01-01 00:00:00+09:00"
    22,2.20,"bbb",2.2,"2.2",False,Infinity,NaN,Infinity,"2017-01-02 03:04:05+09:00"
    333,3.33,"cccc",-3.0,"ccc",True,Infinity,NaN,NaN,"2017-01-01 00:00:00+09:00"
    """)), sep=',')

writer = pytablewriter.MarkdownTableWriter()
writer.from_dataframe(df)
writer.write_table()
```

This is generally caused by a print statement somewhere in your code. By specifying `redirect_stdout` and/or `redirect_stderr` this should be fixed. Note that the previous version had a bug in that code so I would recommend updating to the latest version (3.4.0 or higher) before trying.

http://progressbar-2.readthedocs.org/en/latest/usage.html#combining-progressbars-with-print-output

Example:
```
import time
import progressbar

bar = progressbar.ProgressBar(redirect_stdout=True)
for i in range(100):
    print 'Some text', i
    time.sleep(0.1)
    bar.update(i)
```

> In Exchange Online, the default limits for the Recoverable Items quota are the same as Exchange 2016; a soft limit of 20 GB and a hard limit of 30 GB

We're using the Inbox as a queue to process e-mail attachments. Fetch 1 e-mail --> process --> delete. This use case caused us to hit those recoverable item quota limits easily over a few days and then get stuck there until the recoverable item hold was released (14 days by default).

Regarding your second question, QuerySets support slicing, so you can do something like this:

```python
top_ten_emails = account.inbox.all().order_by('-datetime_received')[:10]
```

To get the first ten emails in your inbox, sorted by `datetime_received` in reverse order. A statement like `first 10 emails` only really makes sense when you specify the field to sort by.

Yeah, `SyncState` is not implemented yet.

You can, however, quite cheaply implement sync functionality by doing something like:

```python
cached_ids = get_ids_from_local_cache(some_emailadress)
fresh_ids = some_account.all().values_list('item_id', 'changekey')
deleted_ids, changed_ids, new_ids = compare_items(cached_ids, fresh_ids)
# Update local cache from these lists
```

The network traffic may be higher, but the code will be easier to reason about, and you won't lose any updates by accident.

Also not sure about parsing ~/.ssh/known-hosts. In some situations that file can be very large. I often have hundreds of hosts in mine. Would you also parse /etc/ssh/ssh_known_hosts? The bit about the ignoring duplication is already there. I always considered the change message duplication to be more of a general django admin thing, since the reversion admin integration works exactly like the built-in admin logging but makes the entries in the object_history view clickable.

As with the standard django interface, you can customise this message by overriding construct_change_message in your admin class.

The django admin integration is a really rather powerful tool. You have to find most of it's features by digging around the source code, however!

In user's custom django.contrib.admin templates he can use global admin site. Global admin site depends on url of global application. And you override global `urlpatters` and local `AdminSite`.

To be clean django-reversion needs to use local subset of `urlpatterns` and local `AdminSite`. Or use global overriding of `urlaptterns` and `AdminSite`.

In the OpenAI Gym example for the lander (https://github.com/CodeReclaimers/neat-python/tree/master/examples/openai-lander), the simulator has 4 exclusive actions, encoded as an integer: 0=do nothing, 1=left thruster, 2=right thruster, 3=main thruster.  To handle this, I use only 'clamped' nodes in the network, so that the outputs are all on the range [-1, 1].  This is meant to be an estimate of the discounted reward that is to be expected from each action, so we take the index of the output with the largest value to be the best action, because the intent is to accumulate a large positive reward.

If you're using NumPy, getting the index of the largest value is conveniently done using the argmax function: action = np.argmax(net.activate(observation))

@Legkw I'm not certain if this is the same issue as Slayoo is experiencing. What you're running into is an actual 404: the path `/items/` doesn't exist, only `/items` and `/items/id`. Note the missing trailing slash in `/items`.

> An important detail to keep in mind is how Flask deals with trailing slashes. The idea is to keep each URL unique so the following rules apply:
> 
> * If a rule ends with a slash and is requested without a slash by the user, the user is automatically redirected to the same page with a trailing slash attached.
> * If a rule does not end with a trailing slash and the user requests the page with a trailing slash, a 404 not found is raised.

See also [this StackOverflow post](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/33241050/trailing-slash-triggers-404-in-flask-path-rule) for more information :)

The iterate function is specifically designed to be used by the `all()` methods on any given endpoint, and only when the user has requested that all objects be returned by setting the `get_all` flag to `True`. The queryparams for `count` and `offset` are removed from the parameters supplied in this case to prevent collisions with the hard-coded values, which are set to prevent flooding the system with requests and instead are designed to grab large numbers of objects per request while still having a reasonable limit of a few thousand records.

Using the `count` and `offset` provided by the user would defeat this purpose, with the result set missing objects if the offset is anything other than 0, and create the potential to flood the system with requests if the `count` is too low. Additionally, the offset in your PR is only used in the first call, meaning that if the offset is greater than 0, there will be duplicated records in the response from the pages that overlap the first call.

@allwefantasy yes, there is a race condition on the last batch if the background TensorFlow process takes a long time to process that batch.  Note that most training loops terminate on `steps` or some other metric, so most folks don't see this... but if you do need the last batch, you can delay the driver for a bit before shutting down (as a quick-and-dirty workaround).  If the TF process takes a variable amount of time on the last batch, that would probably require some synchronization method between the background TF process and the driver (since the data feeding job would be done).

I was wondering whether the library provided a CookieStore class that library users create as a global instance.  That could be passed to the cloudant client constructor:

```
  cookies = CookieStore()
  adapters = ...

  @app.route('/')
   def hello_world():
      client = Cloudant(USERNAME, PASSWORD,
                                    url='https://acct.cloudant.com',
                                    auto_renew=True,
                                    connect=True,
                                    cookie_store=cookies
                                    adapters=adapters)

      # assume we can't/don't want to cache (e.g. Redis) the status doc 
      db = client['mydb']
      doc = ['status'].fetch()
      return doc
```

The cloudant client would attempt to retrieve the session cookie from the cookie store.  If the store doesn't contain a cookie, or it is about to expire within a configurable threshold, the cloudant client would make a call to cloudant to get a new cookie and would save it in the cookie store.

The cookiestore is needed because cloudant python needs somewhere to store/retrieve the session cookie in between executions of hello_world().   The cookieStore will need to be threadsafe and should store the latest session cookie.

Sequential object itself can't be used to generate symbol. With Gluon, you won't have to use symbol. Gluon is generally a more favorable API to use if you are developing new models. When developing new models, you can run the model in imperative mode to immediately examine the outputs, making it very easy to debug. When development finishes, the gluon interface allows caching and reusing the computation graph parts that won't change to achieve close-to-optimal speed.

Assuming that you want to perform object detection on videos, you may want to check out what @zhreshold has been working on with object detection with gluon API in #8639, in which SSD and YOLOv2 are completed, and faster R-CNN and mask R-CNN are on his roadmap.

@Garglesoap  When you call dropout on net, at each training epoch, it drops out random neurons in the layer with a probability of p (0.5 in my example). The first dropout drops out random neurons in the first layer (h1) in the example below, and the second one drops out random neurons in layer h2. By dropping random neurons, dropout prevents overfitting because it forces the network to learn combinations of more robust features instead of only fine tuning weights of features that may be insignificant in a normal neural network. As a result, it might / probably will take longer to train the network. 

Here is my fixed network:
```python
from sklearn.metrics import roc_auc_score, auc
import mxnet as mx

batch_size = 10000
train_iter = mx.io.CSVIter(data_csv="feature_train_v1.csv", data_shape=(50, ),
                   label_csv="label_train_v1.csv", label_shape= (1,), batch_size=batch_size,)

val_iter = mx.io.CSVIter(data_csv = "feature_validate_v1.csv", data_shape=(50, ),
                    label_csv="label_validate_v1.csv", label_shape= (1,), batch_size = batch_size)

data = mx.sym.Variable('data')
net = mx.sym.BatchNorm(data, fix_gamma=True)
net = mx.symbol.Dropout(net, p=0.5)
h1 = mx.sym.FullyConnected(data=net, name='h1', num_hidden=50)
net = mx.sym.Activation(data=h1, act_type="relu")

net = mx.sym.BatchNorm(net, fix_gamma=True)
net = mx.symbol.Dropout(net, p=0.5)
h2 = mx.sym.FullyConnected(data=net, name='h2', num_hidden=25)
net = mx.sym.Activation(data=h2, act_type="relu")

net = mx.sym.BatchNorm(net, fix_gamma=True)
h3 = mx.sym.FullyConnected(data=net, name ='h3', num_hidden=1)

softmax = mx.sym.LogisticRegressionOutput(data=h3, name='softmax') # Calculate the Loss !

model = mx.mod.Module(softmax)

# train the network
model.fit( train_iter,
            eval_data=val_iter,
            eval_metric= roc_auc_score,
            optimizer_params={'learning_rate':0.01, 'momentum': 0.9},
            num_epoch=9)

model.score(val_iter, roc_auc_score)
```

I fixed my error after realizing a few issues. 
* Since it is a classification problem, it helps to use metrics like AUC or cross entropy.
* Try using at least 3 fully connected layers. 
If you are doing a classification problem and are using sklearn's AUC function, it won't work for datasets that don't have enough negative labels (in a binary classification function).

[Issue](https://issues.apache.org/jira/browse/MXNET-852) to add testing to JNI code.

I'll expand on what I said earlier for testing:
Reproducing the issue was fairly straight forward. Using the existing custom operator as a template, I trained and saved a model with a custom operator. Then I attempted to load the model multiple times. On the 3rd attempt a fatal "Operator not found error" was thrown. I then trained another model with 3 of the same custom operators, saved it, then attempted to load it and received the same error.

In both cases, the error was being thrown whenever the upgrade model process called the opPropDel for the 3rd time. The best that I can tell the opPropDel method is only called by the upgradeJSON method when loading a model trained on an older version of MXNet. In this case, there was a counter in the JNI code that on the 3rd time opPropDel was called the custom operator would be deregistered in the native code making all instances of the custom operator unusable and producing a fatal error on the next attempt.

My change was to remove the code that did the deregister and make opPropDel method simply return success (which is what it was already doing up until it hit the counter threshold).

After making this change I loaded the same models that I had trained previously and this time they loaded without issue.

Is there a generic flask template for showing connection info fields? Perhaps its easy to relabel some fields on the form. Personally I think using the username & password is a good idea, because at least then the secret can use the fernet encryption in the meta DB and not be visible in the UI.

Generally with boto/boto3 and other aws sdk's there is a hierarchy of places to retrieve credentials. First check if they are provided as arguments, then check environment variables, then check config file and then fall back to IAM role. I personally use IAM role here because it's the easiest to provision and maintain, and is transparent to the application. The airflow documentation for s3 connection should recommend IAM, assuming the server is in an AWS VPC. IAM is magic.

For this solution, I would recommend following a similar fallback, i.e. use the username & password field, otherwise fall back to the "extra params" field, otherwise let boto do the remaining fallback options and bubble up any exceptions.

I've personally been hacking at the s3hook and saved it as a plugin in my environment because I am running server side encryption (kms) on my s3 buckets and a policy enforcing use of encryption, and the existing s3hook doesn't cater to that. I have it working well, but given current hook uses original boto, and this PR is pending there's no point in me making a PR for this yet.

I've exposed the "headers" parameter of the load_files method which will accept a dict of extra API headers. My approach to this is to let de_json pop all the expected extra params (region, key etc) and then whatever is left throw into the "headers". Specifically I am looking at the 3 that start with "x-amz-server-side-encryption" but theres a whole bunch of possible headers you can send with s3 requests e.g. custom metadata. Hopefully this PR will accommodate that, if not I will wait till it gets merged and look at submitting a change after that.

This introduced a bug when making a copy of an existing object (by setting `pk` to `None`).  Prior to this patch the `create_slug` would be called with `add` being true and then a new slug would be properly created.  This patch short-circuits and ignores `add` and then the copy is saved with the prior slug value (causing a collision).  In fact, it looks like the new method completely ignores the `add` value.

So, this used to work but now doesn't:

```
x = MyModel(name='hi')
x.save()
x.pk = None
x.save()
```

FWIW I don't think we can easily compare Django's model classes with this one, which I think we all have in mind when we talk about `id` fields. But to me the native behavior of ES is to set an id field and there is a option to override it with custom content. The DSL should reflect that, just like other "special" fields such as `_parent` or `_version`.

I think the conceptual disconnect happens when trying to overcome the differences between the list of fields as class attributes and the initialization parameters when creating result objects: The class attributes don't distinguish between native fields and custom fields while the initialization parameter do. So I would guess a solution would be to decide for one level of abstraction:

```python
from esdsl import document, field


class MyDocumentType(document.DocType):
    id = field.Long()
    content = field.String()


result = MyDocumentType(id='123', content'acme')

print(result.id)
```

or:

```python
from esdsl import document, field


class MyDocumentType(document.DocType):
    content = field.String()

    class Meta:
        id = field.Long()


result = MyDocumentType(content'acme', meta={'id': _id})

print(result.meta.id)
print(result._id) # works also for _version etc
```

@Tarrasch Thanks for your appreciation!  I have recently translated https://github.com/17zuoye/luiti README into an English version, which describes the ideas and structure of luiti better. I hope that you can understand it now :)

### Interchange data format
Yes, it breaks `set()`. Actually, I don't often use `set()` as a data interchange format, and I use it mostly just in a single function. Well, I don't know how about other people. I think JSON is a good enough for data exchange, and it's widely used.

### The major bottleneck in MapReduce
Yes, your idea is right and considerate. I'll do a word count MR job on a real YARN cluster.

### the difference between `serialize ` and `internal_serialize`
The first question to consider is that I want to support JSON feature quickly, and don't break related test cases meanwhile. In the default strategy, `serialize` means Python's `str` function, and `internal_serialize` means Python's `repr` function, at the same time, there exists `writer` and `internal_writer` functions. The tricky thing is that str("") return "", and repr("") return "''", so I had to compact with them. Yes, it's not a perfect solution, I think that the MR part of luigi maybe need to be refactored into more decoupled modules.

### Another YARN memory leak problem when there are fewer tasks number.
By the way, in our YARN production environment, luigi seems to have a memory leak problem, it reaches 6.6G threshold value when n_reduce_task is 5, and YARN had to kill it automatically. But if I set n_reduct_tasks to 25 or more, the problem was gone, no other code was changed.

I began to think that if `_map_input` and `_reduce_input` and other related functions don't release the object reference after they are already used, or keep them in a big list or dict until the total task is finished. After a period of investigation, I found `groupby` already guaranteed the data flow is a lazy generator, cause `groupby` requires the data flow is already sorted. So I still can't figure out what's the problem until now, but I know the working mechanism of luigi, and I thought `eval` is a bad thing Intuitively, so that's why I want to support json as a data interchange format in luigi.

@Tarrasch, @dlstadther : Looks like I've gotten as close as I can to getting these tests working. Seems like there's something down inside boto3 that causes a nose.proxy.ImportError -- this is what the Travis CI build is reporting:

```
...
File "/home/travis/build/spotify/luigi/.tox/py33-nonhdfs/lib/python3.3/site-packages/botocore/hooks.py", line 210, in _emit
    response = handler(**kwargs)
  File "/home/travis/build/spotify/luigi/.tox/py33-nonhdfs/lib/python3.3/site-packages/boto3/utils.py", line 61, in _handler
    module = import_module(module)
  File "/home/travis/build/spotify/luigi/.tox/py33-nonhdfs/lib/python3.3/site-packages/boto3/utils.py", line 52, in import_module
    __import__(name)
  File "/home/travis/build/spotify/luigi/.tox/py33-nonhdfs/lib/python3.3/site-packages/boto3/s3/inject.py", line 17, in <module>
    from boto3 import utils
nose.proxy.ImportError: cannot import name utils
```

You'll notice that the stack trace shows that execution is passing through the very module that `nose` is complaining about not existing. Apparently this is something of a known issue with nose, or at least not totally unprecedented.  boto3 certainly works just fine when not run through nose.

I've looked for workarounds for this, but haven't found anything that doesn't require me to either modify the boto3 package or changing the Nose configuration in a way that I don't think I can control directly in the Travis CI build.

For the time being, I'm just going to prevent the tests from running, but I'll leave them in the diff so that, if at a later date someone is able to fix nose so that it doesn't mess up the tests, they will be ready to be activated.

Thanks for letting us know.  Looks like a bug in our doc generation process.  We're assuming that the link keys are unique.  In the raw rst we have:

```
    For information about setting up receipt rule sets, see the `Amazon SES Developer Guide`_ .
```

and then elsewhere in the document:

```
.. _Amazon SES Developer Guide: http://docs.aws.amazon.com/ses/latest/DeveloperGuide/easy-dkim-dns-records.html
```

We keep a map of `link title -> href`, which doesn't work when the same link text is used for multiple links.

We'll get this updated.

From what I can tell, this appears to be something that is ongoing. In the meantime, I did a little digging to find that 'HTTP_ACCEPT' is only requested by the browser, not the prior request made by Splinter.

As a result, if you have a 'TEST_RUN' flag or similar for your test runner settings in Django, I found the below to work in the dispatch method of my view class prior to calling the super method (could be handled in a middleware alternatively).

So far, thing seem to be ok, though I can see how this could be an issue depending on how it is implemented. Nevertheless, this has helped me in the interim.

```python
if settings.TEST_RUN and 'HTTP_ACCEPT' not in request.META:
    return HttpResponse()
```

def get_obj(content, vimtype, name):

@Byron indeed, this is not a bug. To get the correct DIFF, one has always to do:
git diff **PARENT** **CURRENT**

so **NULL_TREE** diff **CURRENT**.

The correct solution is:
```
import git

repo = git.Repo('.')

print('Diff with NULL_TREE')
# this is the null tree, not sure why git.NULL_TREE doesn't work
parent = repo.tree('4b825dc642cb6eb9a060e54bf8d69288fbee4904')
diffs = parent.diff(repo.head.commit.tree)

for diff in diffs:
    print(diff)
    print('type', diff.change_type)
    print()
    print('-' * 10)


print()
print('*' * 10)
print()

print('Diff with parent')
## IMPORTANT: this is inverted, parents vs head commit
diffs = repo.head.commit.parents[0].diff(repo.head.commit)

for diff in diffs:
    print(diff)
    print('type', diff.change_type)
    print()
    print('-' * 10)
```

Reading up on it, indeed it seems like the ASCII protocol (which is what we use, since we haven't implemented the binary protocol) supports unicode keys. At least it never says anything otherwise in the docs, and @jogo proves it above.
> The ASCII protocol uses spaces and newlines. Ensure that neither show up your keys, live long and prosper. Binary protocol does not have this issue.

After looking at what other people do:
- django: uses a self written [smart_str](https://github.com/django/django/blob/master/django/utils/encoding.py#L148-L156) function
- python-memcached uses [binascii](https://docs.python.org/2/library/binascii.html) to transcode unicode to ascii using the 32bit checksum hash:
```
def cmemcache_hash(key):
    return (
        (((binascii.crc32(key) & 0xffffffff)
          >> 16) & 0x7fff) or 1)
serverHashFunction = cmemcache_hash
```
Looking at different hashing solutions in [this great stackoverflow answer](http://softwareengineering.stackexchange.com/questions/49550/which-hashing-algorithm-is-best-for-uniqueness-and-speed), and backed up by @smeder 's advice to another Pinterest engineer, the best hashing for this purpose seems to be [murmur hash](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/mmh3/2.2)

I believe it will be easier to use the murmur hash for unicode keys, and do nothing for ascii friendly keys (ideally we modify as little keys as possible, since that prevents debugging and manually making sense of them.

I am not inclined to merge this pull request, for a few reasons.

* First, I'd like to allow @benosman to merge his own changes: in [a comment on an earlier pull request](https://github.com/theatlantic/django-select2-forms/pull/11#issuecomment-35568503) he had said he wanted to polish the commits before opening a pull request.
* Your commit adding Python 3 support is not backwards compatible with Django 1.4, which is a long-term support release and so I suspect many people using this library are still using it.
* Importing `unicode_literals` is [liable to introduce subtle and tricky regression bugs](http://python-future.org/unicode_literals.html#drawbacks), so as a rule we don't use it when adding Python 3 support. And since Python 3.3 reintroduced the `u""` literal it is no longer necessary. [Here is an example](https://github.com/theatlantic/django-generic-plus/commit/bdf79c395248b457a530c4d588076126329f88c8) of what I think a python 3 support commit should look like. I can set aside some time to do this myself this week or next.
* The templatetags for the static files are unnecessary, in my opinion, when one can just do `form.media`, thanks to Django's [Form Assets](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/dev/topics/forms/media/) functionality. If you find them useful I would recommend using them in your own project, but I don't see a need for them in this library.
* While you might prefer to use a CDN for jQuery and Select2 in your Django project, and there are good reasons for doing so, many people use the `staticfiles` framework to manage their static assets (including us, at *The Atlantic*), along with something like [Django Compressor](http://django-compressor.readthedocs.org/en/latest/) to roll them up. I would be open to a change which introduces a setting, something like `SELECT2_USE_CDN`, which defaults to `False` and so would not break all existing `django-select2-forms` installations.

Lastly, our general preference is for pull requests to be more atomic than this. Each distinct feature or bug fix should really be in its own pull request, so that we have more flexibility to pick and choose parts of the pull request rather than having to reject it outright.

One more goody for all´y´all my robot-friends ;-)

## How To: Change the language of Firefox browser

Find below pure Selenium (Python bindings) & Robot Framework syntax to start Firefox Webdriver browser with specified locale / language.

**Selenium Python bindings code** (which you can try directly in Python interactive shell):

```shell
from selenium import webdriver
firefox_profile = webdriver.FirefoxProfile()
firefox_profile.set_preference("intl.accept_languages", "ru")
browser = webdriver.Firefox(firefox_profile)
browser.get("http://www.youtube.com")
```
This should serve you youtube with a Russian UI (navigation). Change `"ru"` to `"en"` to get youtube with English navigation.

**Robot Framework (selenium2library) code**:

```robot
# keyword definition
*** Keywords ***
| Open URL in Firefox GERMAN |
| | [Arguments] | ${url}
| | [Documentation] | Starts a Firefox browser with specified locale.
| | ${profile}= | Evaluate | sys.modules['selenium.webdriver'].FirefoxProfile() | sys
| | Call Method | ${profile} | set_preference | intl.accept_languages | de
| | Create WebDriver | Firefox | firefox_profile=${profile}
| | Go To | ${url}

# keyword call
*** Test Cases ***
| Open Youtube with German UI
| |  Open URL in Firefox GERMAN | http://www.youtube.com

```

Pass locale as argument:

```robot
*** Keywords ***
| Open URL in Firefox with desired language |
| | [Arguments] | ${url} | ${locale}
| | [Documentation] | Starts a Firefox browser with specified locale.
| | ${profile}= | Evaluate | sys.modules['selenium.webdriver'].FirefoxProfile() | sys
| | Call Method | ${profile} | set_preference | intl.accept_languages | ${locale}
| | Create WebDriver | Firefox | firefox_profile=${profile}
| | Go To | ${url}

*** Test Cases ***
| Open Youtube with Russian UI
| |  Open URL in Firefox with desired language | http://www.youtube.com | ru
```

## Other langues

German:                   "de"
German/Germany:   "de-de"
Spanish:                   "es"
Spanish/Spain:         "es-es"
French:                    "fr"
French/Belgium:      "fr-be"
French/Canada:      "fr-ca"
French/France:       "fr-fr"
Russian:                 "ru"
...

For more look up in Firefox´s preferences / content / Languages

## Final notes
Note that with above approach youtube.com will still serve you content appropriate for your country regardless of locale settings, e.g. in Germany I will see German content even if I start Firefox with Russian locale `"ru"` - only the navigation of the site is in Russian. Also if I use `"es-es"` as locale I get German content and only UI is in Spanish. I guess the served content depends on the settings of the operating system (OS)  or even on the internet network access point. So I think to test a scenario of switching content one will have to switch the OS which is starting the browser (if it depends on the OS) or to use a foreign proxy (if it depends on the network).

@cpoptic The issue is Basemap. They are using an old way (discontinued 4 years ago!) to point pip to the download link that has been slated for removal *years* ago. The old method is finally removed with the new PyPI launch (pypi.org), making the download link no longer visible to PyPI-based package managers, i.e. pip (and by extension, Pipenv).

There are two solutions I can think of:

* Install the package directly, without going through PyPI. Download the source [here](https://sourceforge.net/projects/matplotlib/files/matplotlib-toolkits/basemap-1.0.7/) and run `pipenv install ..../basecamp-1.0.7.tar.gz`.
* Tell Basecamp to upload the tarball (and maybe wheels) to PyPI, and/or provide a clear alternative.

OK, `pip install --upgrade 'git+git://github.com/akszydelko/django-positions.git#egg=positions'` does trigger the issue too. The only solution is really to upgrade setuptools by itself **before** installing the requirements...

As to the reason as why the bug only occurs in some cases, it seems to be due to the way setuptools monkey-patches distutils. More precisely, the sequence of events triggering the bug are:
- setuptools is imported, has a result `distutils.dist.Distribution` has been monkey-patched to `setuptools.dist.Distribution`
- setuptools is uninstalled
- pip's code tries to create a Distribution object, but `setuptools.dist.Distribution` is used

This result in weird behavior in setuptools' code... I have not looked more deeply than that. But if I were to take a guess, I'd say maybe the use of entry-points (dynamic import) for some of setuptools own features is the culprit.

Case in point, applying the following patch to pip fix the issue:
```diff
diff --git i/pip/locations.py w/pip/locations.py
index e598ef10..94c98535 100644
--- i/pip/locations.py
+++ w/pip/locations.py
@@ -12,6 +12,25 @@ from distutils.command.install import install, SCHEME_KEYS  # noqa
 from pip.compat import WINDOWS, expanduser
 from pip.utils import appdirs
 
+# If setuptools is installed, make sure its entry-points are loaded,
+# so we don't end up in a weird state when it's uninstalled but
+# `distutils.dist.Distribution` is still monkey-patched to use
+# `setuptools.dist.Distribution`.
+try:
+    import setuptools
+except ImportError:
+    pass
+else:
+    import pkg_resources
+    for ep_type in (
+        'distutils.setup_keywords',
+        'distutils.commands',
+        'setuptools.installation',
+        'egg_info.writers'
+    ):
+        for ep in pkg_resources.iter_entry_points(ep_type):
+            if ep.dist.project_name == 'setuptools':
+                ep.load()
 
 # Application Directories
 USER_CACHE_DIR = appdirs.user_cache_dir("pip")
```

I don't know if that patch would be considered as acceptable for inclusion in pip, but this could prevent those kind of weird bugs (and allow for maybe some minor simplifications in setuptools' code).

The problem is that the JSON-API cannot support it because we need one request per plone site because of things such as the site-hook (`zope.component.hooks.getSite`).

We could add support in the `bin/upgrade` command though, but this can be also done quite easy be using the `sites` command, e.g.:

```shell
$ ./bin/upgrade sites --json | python -c 'import json,sys; print "\n".join([site["path"] for site in json.loads(sys.stdin.read())])' | xargs -I % bin/upgrade list -p --site %
```

I therefore think we should not add this feature to ftw.upgrade but update fix it in the scripts using `bin/upgrade`.

> I can see potentially adding a partition_offsets argument to commit_offsets so that this logic would not have to be limited only to self._partitions

If I'm understanding correctly, I don't think this would work. The other running consumers wouldn't know that you've done this and would just keep consuming at their current offsets. When they commit they'd overwrite whatever new offset you'd written out for them. Thinking on it this way, I think that forcibly taking ownership of all of the partitions is the only real way to reset their offsets without having your work just be undone again.

One saving grace is that this doesn't have to be atomic (for my purposes anyway) but looking at the [consumer rebalance algorithm](https://kafka.apache.org/documentation.html#impl_consumerrebalance) it doesn't look like "insisting" on a particular ownership result is a thing that's built in.
Yes this fix resolves it.

+1  without this fix, only the first partition/group  is having it's current offset updated.   This  causes a balanced_consumer to consume from only one partition (ignoring the rest if they exist), when reset_offset_on_start=True is used.

I encountered this bug as well when trying to use this function to calculate maximum intensity values using a saturated psf. If you would like I can try and reproduce the bug using different input but below is the proper solution to just fix the code so this can never happen.

The solution is to use machine epsilon:

`eps = np.finfo(image.dtype).eps`
`x = convolve_method(im_deconv, psf, 'same')`
`np.place(x, x==0, eps)`
`relative_blur = image / x + eps`

It's related to non-throwing destructors in c++11, which gcc4.7+ apparently adheres to. 
https://stackoverflow.com/questions/42976461/exception-in-destructor-c
The proposed global solution is not optimal, though.

A) Best option would be not to throw (aka NTA_CHECK) in the destructor `~Output()` at all, although the comment explicitely mentions that we are aware of the "bad practice" but we need to check that. 

B) I'm proposing a fix that only allows the behavior locally.

import sqlalchemy.pool as pool
