We don't have RTFD for many packages still, which is the ultimate goal before releasing Invenio 3.0 final... So @inveniosoftware/developers let's join [I3B-API-Docs](https://github.com/search?utf8=%E2%9C%93&q=user%3Ainveniosoftware+milestone%3AI3B-API-Docs) [kanban](https://waffle.io/inveniosoftware/invenio?milestone=I3B-API-Docs) to get there faster! 

> I would have found it extremely confortable and convenient if there would have been a README.sh directly printed there

I really wonder how frequent is the use case of "people-expecting-to-see-something-displayed-inline-when-browsing-GitHub-directories". How do people know that if they click on "examples" that they would get some help directly printed there? And where do we stop? Would people expect to click on "tests" directory to see a directly printed guide on how to run and write tests?  Would they expect to click on "docs" and see direct link to RTFD? Would they expect to see a Transifex guide after clicking on "translations" directory? And what about clicking inside our source code directories? How many READMEs would we need to fit this "browsing around" use case?

In my eyes, all these "getting started" needs are better addressed in a more scalable way by (i) boot camp exposure to package structure for new developers (already being done, but IRL only),  accompanied by (ii) currently subdocumented [package anatomy](http://invenio.readthedocs.io/en/latest/architecture/package-anatomy.html), which we should further expand upon #3657 #3658. Richer documentation would address these needs in a more comprehensive manner.

> Then again, I can read the makefile 

... this is what I was thinking that a developer instinct would do. :smile:  It's true that it's "one click away" when browsing GitHub, but is it really such a big deal?

P.S. Again, let me repeat that we could have both `examples/README` and `examples/Makefile`... I simply find it a bit redundant, especially if README is more or less going to point to Makefile...

You just make a custom payload handler, e.g.:

```
from rest_framework_jwt.utils import jwt_payload_handler

def jwt_custom_payload_handler(user):
    payload = jwt_payload_handler(user)
    payload['role'] = 'xyz'
    return payload
```

Then in your settings you just need:

```
JWT_AUTH['JWT_PAYLOAD_HANDLER'] = 'path.to.your.jwt_custom_payload_handler',
```

Thanks for raising this issue.

Some brainstorming around the possible ways to approach this:
1. Pass in a formatter (as you suggested)
- Do we want a way for the formatter to fall back to the built in serializer?
2. Allow Fire's serialization to be silenced.
- Since the result is returned in the output of Fire already, the client can handle the serialization themselves.
3. Let objects implement something like __str__ or __firestr__ which fire's builtin serialization method will use.
4. Improve Fire's built in serializer, but don't allow custom serializers.
- There's definitely value in consistency. E.g. custom serialization probably wouldn't be compatible with piping outputs of one fire command as inputs to another (this feature may just be a pipe dream though :P).

Related note:
- One potential upcoming change to the serializer is that containers made purely of literals will be handled differently than containers with non-literals.
As an example, a dict of literals will be serialized in the current way (one key-value pair per line), whereas a dict with functions in it may get shown as a usage string, similar to how an object with properties is shown.

I think this is finally ready for review!

Summary of additions/changes:

- Added `ChromaticRealGalaxy`, the chromatic version of `RealGalaxy`.   This object uses multi-band HST images of galaxies to produce realistic `ChromaticObject`s, including realistic SEDs and color gradients.
- Added `CovarianceSpectrum`, which is a generalization of a power spectrum (or generalization of a `CorrelatedNoise` object in galsim-speak).  Mostly an implementation detail, as it gets turned into a `CorrelatedNoise` object when an image of a `ChromaticRealGalaxy` gets drawn.
- Added `drawKImage` to `ChromaticObject` and `BaseCorrelatedNoise`.
- Added `wcs` keyword to `GSObject.drawKImage`.  Similarly, added support for non-`PixelScale` wcses to `InterpolatedKImage`.  This was mostly to handle Fourier transforms of non-square real space images.
- Reorganized the packaged bandpasses and SEDs.  They now live in the $galsim_meta_data/bandpasses and $galsim_meta_data/SEDs.  Also updated the bandpass and SED download scripts, and added script for HST AEGIS and CANDELS filters.  Pointed LSST bandpass download script to modern repository.
- Added test_CRG.py and test_CRG_noise.py to devel/modules/chromatic_real_galaxy/.  These are useful test/demo scripts, but too slow to use as unit tests.
- Added devel/modules/CGNotes.tex which goes through the math behind `ChromaticRealGalaxy` and `CovarianceSpectrum`.
- Added a small test catalog (5 objects) for testing `ChromaticRealGalaxy`.
- Added documentation to `RealGalaxy` and `ChromaticRealGalaxy` clarifying that methods like `withFlux` set the postage stamp flux, not the galaxy flux, and that the two can be quite different if the signal-to-noise ratio of the source image(s) is low.
- changed return type of `RealGalaxyCatalog.getPSF` and `RealGalaxyCatalog.getGal` to be `GSObject`s instead of `Image`s.  Use `getPSFImage` and `getGalImage` instead for getting `Image`

Part of the issue is caused by the `transform: translateZ(0);` hack, forcing webkit to render hundreds of layers on the GPU, and keep them on VRAM, this is a really bad practice and why premature optimisations like that are bad. Removing that css property improves scrolling significantly for me.

You should not force rendering engine to create layers unnecessary, because you want to let it raster and keep a smaller in system memory image instead of large amount of layers on video ram (which is more scarce than system ram)

@Crissov I want to thank you for your big effort here and in all other issues where you are providing the standard unicode point of the request. ❤️ 

This PR is quite impressive and it is a very good starting point for a brainstorming about standard unicode points

This choice is far out from my tasks, but I can definitely point out a couple of things:
1. The `.svg` file is automatically generated from the `.otf` file via font squirrel. So, standard unicode points should be added to the `.otf` (and we are not accepting PR on that file, mostly because of its binary nature);
2. This is _probably_ a breaking change. I know that we could update our build scripts to reflect this change, but we don't know what other users may be doing with that `.yml`. So, the best thing we can do is to consider another way of adding standard unicode points to the .yml file, leaving `unicode:` as it is.  I can't actually think of a better name, maybe just a simple array `std_unicode`.
  E.g. 
  ```yml
  - name:       Check
    id:         check
    unicode:    f00c
    std_unicode:
      - 2713 # ✓
      - 2714 # ✔
  ```

Why you set `ttl` so small?
It MUST be at least twice bigger as `loop_wait`, better three times.
Also, if you change `loop_wait` and `ttl` you have to adjust `retry_timeout` parameter, which is 10 by default.
General rule is: `loop_wait+2*retry_timeout < ttl`

Very small ttl and retry_timeout is very questionable, cluster wont be able to tolerate short network lags.

I developed my own workaround to retrieve the allowed values of a custom field. My implementation is specific to a hard-coded fieldname called c_Product, but the code can be easily generalized if needed. The method accepts a reference to a Rally object.

~~~~
def getAllowedProductValues(rally):

    # Get the list of attributes/fields available for a User Story AKA HierarchicalRequirement
    schemaItem = rally.contextHelper.getSchemaItem('HierarchicalRequirement')
    if not schemaItem:
        print 'Internal Error: Could not retrieve a list of User Story fields.'
        return None

    # Find our attribute in the schema
    for attrib in schemaItem.Attributes:
        if attrib.ElementName == 'c_Product':
            # Retrieve the attribute's collection of allowed values
            allowedValues = rally.getCollection(attrib.AllowedValues)
            if allowedValues.errors:
                print 'Internal Error: Could not retrieve available Products, error code: %d.\n' % allowedValues.status_code
                return None
            if allowedValues.resultCount == 0:
                # This should never happen
                print 'Internal Error: No Rally products found.'
                return None

            #Return a list of allowed values in lowercase
            return [allowedValue.StringValue.lower() for allowedValue in allowedValues]

    return None
~~~~

Sur les prochaines étapes, je pense qu'il faut distinguer l'agenda "centralisé" lié à cette PR, des différentes adaptations des packages divers de l'univers `openfisca`.

En principe, le versionning propre nous permet de laisser chaque acteur libre du moment où il choisira d'investir du temps pour effecteur la migration de son package. Cette migration devra être facilitée par une documentation claire, et éventuellement par un soutien ponctuel des auteurs de changements importants sur `core`.

La roadmap "centralisée" serait donc pour moi la suivante:

- [x] S'assurer que la proposition actuelle passe au moins tous les tests de `france`, afin de prouver qu'elle peut gérer les situations variées du monde openfisca.
- [ ] Documenter clairement les changements principaux, et les nouvelles modalités d'usages.
- [ ] Merger la PR sur core pour éviter le stock et acter que c'est la direction prise, pour que tous les travaux futurs se basent sur cette version.
- [ ] Au bout d'un temps à définir, déprécier l'ancienne syntaxe. Ce temps peut être sujet à discussion, fixé à l'avance ou pas, cela dépend de la stratégie de dépréciation d'openfisca.

Dans l'idéal, on aurait 2 avant 3, mais nous n'avons pas encore d'outil de documentation technique, donc on risque de n'avoir qu'une documentation que partielle dans un premier temps.

This is indeed much better than reforms.

The main issue / limitation is, as you've all identified, that there is still a need to add the extension to the main codebase, thus not reducing contributor experimentation friction as it could. One way could be to `.gitignore` the extensions folder, and let each contributor maintain her list of extensions on her own, through whichever mechanism is the best fit. This is probably the most scalable.

Another way could be to look up in `../openfisca-extensions` if the preference is put on convention. If the preference is put on configuration, then add a config flag. But we very weary of where to set this up.

I would recommend ignoring the extensions folder and leaving to the user to define her strategy.

@kirknorth By default the `dealias_unwrap_phase` function verifies that the **unwrap_unit parameter** is valid.  In the case of the 'sweep' (and 'volume') this check consists of a verification that the sweep were collected sequentially, ie no jumps in azimuth/elevation angles for PPI/RHI scans.  This does not  have an option for vpt scans ([see line 276 in unwrap.py](https://github.com/ARM-DOE/pyart/blob/ead72e73ab440acff92c206fdaa7c28b2fb82a97/pyart/correct/unwrap.py#L276)) but probably should.  A check which verifies that the time coordinate does not run backwards is best, I'll add it momentarily.

For the time being this verification can be skipped by setting the **skip_checks** parameter to True which disable all verification.

If you choose to implement recursive delete:
1. It could take an unbounded amount of time depending on how many objects there are in the bucket.
2. You'll need to handle concurrent writes and return a clear error condition in this case (optionally, implementing retries).
3. Eventual listing consistency can cause errors in the case of non-concurrent writes.  In other words, if the an application serially uploads and object, waits for success, then deletes the bucket, it's possible the object won't be present in the listing at the time that you but that the delete will fail.

I don't have a strong opinion as to whether you should implement this or not; if you do, make sure you document the behavior clearly, as well as the errors you'll raise in the case of the above failures.

n my current solution, I added mutation class and the new multi rows mutation function as a method of the Table class.
Pros: 1) Easy to use - mutations can be specified outside of rows; 2) No need to construct Row instances. Original RPC call works on a level of row keys.
Cons: 1) new abstraction 2) The Row class should be refactored and work with mutations 3) Mutations for every row should be the same.

Alternative solution. Same new table method. In a method body - extract rows keys/mutations and build request. 
Pros: 1) No new abstractions
Cons: 1) Extracting row key from row object 2) Not so easy to use - Rows should be constructed first.

The second way, from my point of view, is closer to the current  implementation and would prefer to extended Row class a little. Even with a small overhead for extracting keys from rows.

Any cons?

I investigated the problem a little bit further and found out that lookup_types year, month, day, week_day, hour, minute and second causes problems. `Column.search` and `DateColumn.prep_search_value` functions together produce column queries that are illegal. They produce queries that tries to compare e.g. day to datetime.date object (e.g. `('start_date__year', datetime.date(2017, 4 20)`). However it should compare day to int (or something else which can be converted to int).

As a temporary fix I allowed only excat, in and range lookup_types in `DateColumn `and `DateTimeColumn `(`lookup_types = ('exact', 'in', 'range')`).

I think that the primary cause of the problem lies in the line:
`date_obj = datetime.strptime(test_term, test_format)`
which tries to extract year, month, day etc. from test_term string. I will never succeed because it test_format is never legal date format string (e.g. '%Y-%m-%d %H:%M". `datetime.strptime` expects to get complete data format string and not one part (%Y, %m, %d etc.) at the time.

Regards,

I'm going to default this to allow for now; we can have a discussion about changing that default in the future.

It turns out that "restricted mode" is a thing; if `__builins__` is not the same as the global `__builtins__` [1] (as is the case here), some constructs stop being available to the interpreter. In particular, the `file()` constructor is banned, so no files can be opened if `__builtins__` is changed. While this is probably good (people should definitely not be opening files in BUILD files), it's a more breaking change than I'm comfortable quietly making.

1: https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/2.7/Include/frameobject.h#L58

I have some questions, mostly due to my relative ignorance of Node...

1. What is node_scope for? Is this an NPM concept?
2. Why specify these dependencies in `package.json` and not as regular BUILD file deps? Is this for compatibility with NPM/Yarn?
3. What, in fact, is the relationship between these various build steps and NPM? At what points are we invoking NPM? For example, when you say "During resolution phase, all third party packages will be installed first", is that done via NPM?
4. Where will these packages and sources be installed to? Something under `.pants.d` presumably? But note that multiple tasks must not act on the same output directory. In JVM and Python-land a task consumes the outputs of previous tasks as products, but AFAICT that won't work well with NPM. But in this case it sounds (and I could be wrong) like we'd be creating a "workspace" that multiple tasks all operate on? I don't think we have an example of that today, and we'd need to figure that one out.

@kwlzn and I have had some discussion around this and one potential solution is to write a setuptools plugin that injects a local version identifier into the wheel name during setup.py execution so as to properly use the new artifact. Another solution could be to modify the caching strategy to give local py_dist wheels special treatment to avoid this. 

@jsirois So you are saying that the resolver will use pypi if that exists over a local find-links repo? The current implementation creates a synthetic req lib for the local wheel and gives it the .pants.d path as its `PythonRequirement` `repository` attribute, resolving from there. Based on my testing, it looks to me like the resolver uses the find-links repo over pypi (for the case where I name `fasthello_test==1.0.0` in setup.py to be a real package on pypi like `flask=0.12`).

> Should I also add unit tests around this new behavior? I'm not sure what type of test would be meaningful - something like asserting that you can't write unicode to a binary file seems repetitive to me as Python provides this functionality and error handling.

Rather than adding new tests, getting the existing tests running in Python3 is probably the highest priority. If we don't already have functionality exercising the case, then it's a "tree falling in the forest" situation.

    },

> Could be the case that the jar included statically as jpmml-converter-1.2.3 in SkLearn2PMML was not generated from the right code base?

I've experienced a situation, where the `resources` directory of an installed sklearn2pmml package contains several versions of the same library JAR file (eg. JPMML-Converter versions 1.2.0, 1.2.3). The JVM then adds the first out-of-date JAR file (eg. 1.2.0) to classpath, and ignores the second up-to-date JAR file (eg. 1.2.3). Such classpath conflicts may lead to all sorts of unexpected outcomes, starting with JVM class loader/verifier errors and ending with incorrectly generated PMML markup.

Could it be that you had multiple versions of the JPMML-Converter library in the `resources` directory? This could happen if you install a new version of the sklearn2pmml package, without properly uninstalling the old version first.

Anyway, a good "fix" for library errors is to 1) delete the `sklearn2pmml` subdirectory from the `site-packages` directory of your active Python installation, and 2) install a the latest version of the sklearn2pmml package from GitHub.

I looked up this travis env variable:
```
TRAVIS_COMMIT_RANGE: The range of commits that were included in the push or pull request.
```

TBH, I don't understand well how it's determined which commits are in the commit range, but it appears that it includes *more* than just the commits listed on the PR. For instance if your branch is behind the target then it will contain subsequent commits as well... which means that potentially it could include commits that have the string `[ setup ]` in the message *even if* your PR doesn't include such a commit, AFAICT. For the same reason, if someone changed a file that matches one of the targets then your PR could run the build tests even if *you* didn't change those files since it will be in the changeset of the commit range.

Not sure if it's possible to work around this. What do you think? Does it even matter? My only thought is that it sort of gives the wrong impression of *when* the conditional test is run. If in reality we can't control it precisely, then I think we might as well just run such a test *all* the time. With the sdist in particular, why not build and install from it every time by default?

Hello Charles,

Thanks for the report! It sounds a bit like there's some terminology overload going on here. The `client_id`, `client_secret`, and `refresh_token` keys in the configuration file refer to your OAuth 2.0 configuration. In contrast, the `client_customer_id` is a separate concept referred to in AdWords--it is what identifies the AdWords account for which you intend to make the API call. There should be a separate configuration key labeled `client_customer_id` in the configuration file under `adwords`. As an example, it exists in the sample configuration file that can be found [here](https://github.com/googleads/googleads-python-lib/blob/master/googleads.yaml#L10).

Note that, despite being an optional field, it is required for many API calls.

Regards,
Mark

> I'd like to reiterate my point above, especially considering that a similar PR was merged in the reference ufoLib implementation, so there's now a discrepancy between the way the latter and the ufoNormalizer treat float strings: unified-font-object/ufoLib#20

ufoLib is not a normalizer. The normalization guidelines are not requirements. Editors can do whatever they want within the parameters of the UFO specification.


The point of the normalizer is to take output from two environments and ensure that they are consistent to a certain level of precision. Here's an example:

- Designer A uses Editor 1 to create a component in glyph "A" with a transformation with an x scale of 0.1.
- Designer A commits UFO to a repository.
- Designer B pulls the UFO from the repository.
- Designer B edits glyph "B" in Editor 2.
- Designer B commits the change to the repository.

Unbeknownst to Designers A and B, Editor 2 writes over all GLIF files, regardless of if the represented glyph has changed or not, *and* the language that Editor 2 is written in outputs floats differently that the language used by Editor 1. So, instead of "0.1" the transformation is now "0.10000000000000001". The designers then have a noisy, pointless diff. Mutliply this by N number of components in N number of fonts and it's a lot of unnecessary noise. Insert normalization between the editors and the repository and these differences are abstracted away.

I completely understand your desire for roundtripping unlimited precision in the abstract. I'm a *precision-to-the-max* guy myself. However, I'm not sure that the increased precision for some edge cases outweighs the effect of increasing the float length for all UFOs. I know that `repr` will reduce to only significant digits, but that's still potentially a lot of digits to represent precision that won't ultimately be relevant. I could see the benefit of arbitrary precision being an argument when calling the normalizer, but I don't think it's a good idea to force this onto everyone all the time.

Perhaps precision could be specified like this:

```python
normalizeUFO("path/to/my.ufo", floatPrecision=10) # the default
normalizeUFO("path/to/my.ufo", floatPrecision=1000) # mega precise!
normalizeUFO("path/to/my.ufo", floatPrecision=None) # use repr
```

This would need to be worked into the guts of the normalizer, but that shouldn't be too hard.

Just to reiterate my position on the overall outlook for the UFO: I'm significantly more concerned about keeping the main UFO workflow working smoothly for daily common use. If an edge case can be handled without causing unnecessary effects in common use cases, that's great. If not, I prefer opt-out arguments than can be used by designers seeking specific handling of unusual situations. To be honest, I'm seeing more and more edge case handling and invalid data ignoring being pushed into the workflow at the potential expense of most users and it's making me *very* nervous. I'd like to try to make these slopes a lot less slippery rather than more.

I'm not directly using txaio. As mentioned, Autobahn's `ApplicationSession.onJoin` will currently not log an error if a generator is returned, but won't execute anything as well. I'm sure there will be many more places with similiar behaviour. Instead of checking this on every single occasion, I think the best solution would be to directly solve this in `txaio.as_future` even if this means some small syntactic inconveniences for those very rare cases were generators are actually expected as results.

So first regarding asyncio: We solely work with twisted so I'm not really an expert on this area. Although while I could replicate your described behaviour of `iscoroutine()`, I noticed that `iscoroutinefunction()` is able to differentiate just fine between:

```
def test(): # will return False
    yield 1

@asyncio.coroutine
def test2(): # will return True
    yield 2
```

Now regarding twisted: I don't even think that it is required to replace `maybeDeferred`, a crude implementation of `as_future` could be as simple as:

```
def as_future(func, *args, **kwargs):
    d = maybeDeferred(func, *args, **kwargs)
    if d.called:
        def cb(result):
            if isinstance(result, _GeneratorWrapper):
                return result.gen

            if inspect.isgenerator(result):
                raise Exception("function returned a generator. Did you forget to decorate your function with @inlineCallbacks?")

            return result

        d.addCallback(cb)

    return d
```

The documentation from asyncio is not really clear and I ended up writing something that suffered from a "race condition". It would only work half the time and I had to use global variables. The solution was simple:

```python
class MyComponent(ApplicationSession):
    def __init__(self, config=None):
        # Or call using super()
        ApplicationSession.__init__(self, config)
        loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
        loop.add_signal_handler(signal.SIGINT, self.exit_cleanup)
        loop.add_signal_handler(signal.SIGTERM, self.exit_cleanup)
```

> Some users may have different setups

This is the primary reason I created this PR. PostgreSQL setups can vary greatly, so I wanted a "universal" setup that could be used on every computer to run the deis unit tests.

This way people can run:

``` console
$ make -C controller postgres
$ make -C controller test
```

And know that they are running their tests in a setup that will work, rather than try to install and configure their local PostgreSQL database to work with the tests.

I created this based with the same idea as `make dev-registry`, a simple way to get a complicated component working.

This could also simply the current CI setup, which currently has a complicated procedure for installing and configuring PostgreSQL.

I understand this is not a straightforward decision, and I don't have any hard feelings if this isn't accepted :heart::smile:. I found it useful for development and thought others might as well.

Some more information, that might be related to this. 

We use dockerfiles for deployment. Deployment is done through a script that that runs "git push deis master" inside several project folders. It was assumed that, if a project is already up to date in deis remote it would be not deployed and just returned with a message "everything is up to date". 

But that does no seem to be happening, I just ran a ```deis releases``` command in a app and found many releases with same git hash. Which means, its updating the apps, again and again - even when they are up to date. 

Sample ```deis releases``` output below:

```
v5      2015-11-05T11:56:07UTC  vinay deployed 0818d96
v4      2015-11-04T11:25:59UTC  vinay deployed 0818d96
v3      2015-11-02T07:01:45UTC  vinay deployed 0818d96
v2      2015-10-30T09:35:40UTC  vinay deployed 0818d96
v1      2015-10-30T09:33:52UTC  vinay created initial release
```

So to explain this more clearly (thanks @technosophos), the health checker indicates whether the container is ready to service requests. If it fails, publisher will not publish it to etcd. The default state before the initial delay is `Down`. The state for a container when no health check is provided is assumed to be `Up` (assuming the container is actually "up"). This will run forever, continuously checking if the container is up or down, publishing and unpublishing as needed. This is the *Liveness* check.

Now, here's where the controller comes into play: the controller is the unit that deploys and recycles containers as needed, since it manages an application's lifecycle. Because of this, it needs to perform a *Readiness* check after an initial delay. For Heroku, this is at 60 seconds, which then if the container is not listening on the expected port then the dyno is killed off. For us, we need to check to ensure the new release is up and responding. If it is down, then we need to kill it and keep the old containers.

> I think we should probably try an incremental backoff strategy that starts with 10% of the HEALTHCHECK_INITIAL_DELAY, then 50%, then 100%.

That sounds like a happy medium for the liveness checker.

> Wouldn't it be better to filter this, at least by application, and possibly by version?

Sure. The idea of a global list is to show all containers being published on a single host (since publisher works on a host-by-host basis), but I can add query filtering.

I'm thinking about whether or not we should try to check the validity of the tokens.

It's obviously nice for the client to detect bad tokens and prompt for replacement in that specific case.
However, native apps can't use introspect, and under globus/globus-sdk-python#97 we discussed the fact that the token validation call isn't meant for clients to use at all, but for resource servers. If we think of Native Apps which want to check on their token validity as a valid and necessary use-case for that call, then that would be the best bet.

Barring some change to give us a long-term supported path for native apps to check validity (either a change in introspect or in our thinking about validate, since we don't want to use a call that may stop working in the future), not checking and assuming any tokens seen are valid seems best.

If I had to guess, I'd say that `DataPublicationClient` is probably relying on an undefined behavior which has been _improved_ to be an error (yes, there is an element of irony to that). Namely, #247 (released in v1.3.0) fixes the fact that unmatched/invalid service names would give clients a `None` URL, and an error at use-time (vs instantiation time).

The fix, luckily, is simple: as of v1.2.2 all clients accept `base_url` as a keyword argument, which lets you programatically set the URL for a client.

IIUC, conda first downloads the package tarball from whichever channel you ask it to download from and it is kept in the directory `/path/to/root/conda/environment/pkgs` or in `$HOME/.conda/pkgs`. However, this can be overridden by specifying a different directory using the config parameter `pkgs_dirs`. So you can do:
```
conda config --prepend pkgs_dirs /path/to/custom/dir/pkgs
```
Now, this directory will have all the tarballs and their extracted contents. Each package tarball has the file `info/files` which contains the list of all files which are supposed to installed in the activated conda environment. Now, conda attempts to hard link all of these files into the current environment. However, if there is a file `info/has_prefix` in the package tarball, then those files will be patched based on the string provided in that file. These files will not have the same inode as the one in the package cache directory as they need to have environment-path specific strings in them.

Now, looking at an example (on installation of the conda package: curl):

The files to be installed into the environment are:
```
(root) [thanos@titan ~]# cat $CONDA_PREFIX/pkgs/curl-7.52.1-0/info/files
bin/curl
bin/curl-config
include/curl/curl.h
include/curl/curlbuild.h
include/curl/curlrules.h
include/curl/curlver.h

Ilan as discussed I'm *incredibly* supportive and completely on board.  I also want to be careful and deliberate about how we make the transition though.  Actually, this very "feature" is on my working roadmap as one of the distinguishing features for the conda 5.0 release.

I see that you've taken special care to start your client and scheduler in Python 3.  Perhaps you need to take the same care with your workers?  When you ssh into your different hosts and run `dask-worker` perhaps in that ssh session you are still using your system Python 2 installation.  When the Python 2 worker tries to connect up to the Python 3 scheduler it fails, which is why you don't see anything.

One solution to this would be to put the `source activate py35` line in your `.bash_profile` or `.bashrc` files.  This way all sessions, including ssh sessions, would run under python 3.5 by default.

Thanks for reporting this.  It is quite likely that there needs to be a higher timeout for this operation.  Have you tried running with a higher timeout value than the default of 30 seconds?

You could add the `--debug` flag to see more.

7200 seconds is extremely short for a snapshot.  I do not recommend using the `--request_timeout` flag as it is most commonly used in conjunction with—but opposition to—the `--timeout` flag.  `--timeout` is for the client timeout (the connection to Elasticsearch), where `--request_timeout` should be less than the `--timeout` flag.  `--request_timeout` is used to force Elasticsearch to return in the event a request is long running.  It's not for managing a client-level timeout.

Those things said, it looks like you have almost exactly 4 minutes between creating the client and the timeout, which is less than the client timeout of 21600 _and_ the request_timeout of 7200.  Chances are you're hitting a gateway timeout (504) error, [described in this FAQ](https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/client/curator/current/_q_snapshots_seem_to_be_working_why_am_i_getting_literal_snapshotmissingexception_literal_messages.html).  You can see for sure if you use the `--debug` flag.

Disk space is only part of the equation.  It's actually the least important part.  Each shard in Elasticsearch comes with a management cost.  The master node(s) have to ensure that all nodes keep a current cluster state.  The index cache portion defaults to 10% of the allocated heap.  Each active shard wants 250MB of that 10%, and each inactive shard wants 4M, and you have some indices with 40+0 shards. If a node cannot keep all of the shards in the 10% with these values, it begins to compress them.  This compression slows down indexing and is tied to overburdening of the cluster.  In fact, with too many shards per index, I've seen those values compressed to the point that there was only 47M of cache available for each active shard and 750K for each inactive shard.  At or around that point, Elasticsearch stopped indexing outright, though search still worked.

This is why I asked how many nodes you have, and how many shards per node.  Also important to add to the equation is how big your heap is on each node.  The sheer number of indices and shards you have could be leading to this pain point.

Currently there is no direct way to inject your own headers. What I recommend is to subclass the connection class and modify the headers there:
```
from elasticsearch import Urllib3HttpConnection, Elasticsearch
class MyConnection(Urllib3HttpConnection):
    def __init__(*args, **kwargs):
        extra_headers = kwargs.pop('extra_headers', {})
        super(MyConnection, self).__init__(*args, **kwargs)
        self.headers.update(extra_headers)

es = Elasticsearch(connection_class=MyConnection, extra_headers={'Any': 'header'})
```

Unfortunately there is no easy way to do this on a per-api basis.

These statements are a bit confusing to me also. 

The deployment username is a concept that's tied to your AAD user that you use to login the portal or the az cli. The relationship is 1:1 between deployment users and AAD users. The only reason deployment users exist is because some popular publishing tools don't support fancy AAD login requirements things like FTP and older versions of git. Therefore, we give you the option of specifying a deployment userName and password that you can use with these tools. These creds are not per resource group, nor are they per subscription, nor are they per app. They are global to any your AAD user has access to. And they are unique per AAD user.

Hi Gabriele,

The assignment list extension is only for when you are
distributing/collecting assignments from your students using nbgrader, so
in your case you wouldn't want to use it. The best option here would be to
disable the assignment list extension.

> If the latter, am I still guaranteed everything keeps working given that
- here I'm quoting from user documentation - "*To work properly, the
assignment list, formgrader, and validate extensions require both the
nbextension and serverextension*"?

Yes, everything will still work correctly. The wording of the documentation
is perhaps a bit confusing---what it means is that each of the assignment
list, formgrader, and validate extensions have two parts to them: a
nbextension and a serverextension. So to make sure each of the assignment
list/formgrader/validate extensions work, you have to make sure both the
corresponding nbextension and serverextension are enabled (so there are 6
separate pieces you could enable/disable total). If you want to disable an
extension (for example the assignment list extension) you should disable
both the nbextension and serverextension parts for it.

Hope that helps to clear things up!

Cheers,
Jess

There are three (or more!) parts to write a specification for. The backend/filesystem layout, how a frontend requests them (is it direct, is it a url path in the notebook server `/kernelspec/ir/static/...`, is it per running kernel), as well as how it ends up in the notebook document (per cell, metadata across the notebook).

> The frontend would need to implement a deduplicator to only include this code only once per document and handle removals if reevaluation of cells is possible

Definitely. It seems like the approach you outlined for knitr is great for us all to think about in terms of the notebook @janschulz. I prefer a URL based approach to asset requiring up until I'm offline (which is fairly frequent). A big draw to our current format is that it works without having to be connected to the wider internet all the time.

Since I also care about Hydrogen, Thebe, and other frontends beyond the notebook, my primary interest is in getting the backend specification done across kernels. One approach is for kernel spec directories to contain static assets:

```
├── kernels
│   ├── ir
│   │   ├── kernel.json
│   │   ├── logo-64x64.png
│   │   └── static
```

What belongs in static I'm unsure of. Let's say we operated with npm packages underneath:

```
├── kernels
│   ├── ir
│   │   ├── kernel.json
│   │   ├── logo-64x64.png
│   │   └── static
│   │       ├── node_modules
│   │       │   └── d3
│   │       └── package.json
```

While this would work well for node based frontends ([hydrogen](https://github.com/nteract/hydrogen), [nteract](https://github.com/nteract/nteract), [sidecar](https://github.com/nteract/sidecar)), it would not work well on the main notebook or any other remote environment without also specifying how we do bundling (webpack, browserify, etc.).

> to make this all first class it seems like jupyter would need a way to specify kernel configuration

The KernelSpecManager and KernelManager classes are the implementations of finding and launching kernels, respectively. These are swappable for alternate implementations via the `kernel_manager_class` and `kernel_spec_manager_class` configurables on NotebookApp.

I just put together [pexnb](https://github.com/minrk/pexnb) which provides a KernelSpecManager that works with PEX and tells the notebook server to use it by default.

You should be able to build a notebook env with pex via:

    pex notebook pexnb -m pexnb -o ./jupyter_notebook.pex
    $PWD/jupyter_notebook.pex

It has the assumptions:

1. pex file is launched via absolute path (otherwise the subprocess cannot find the executable, since pex appears to throw away this information by changing to a temporary directory)
2. you don't want other kernels available (misses some of the point of Jupyter, but if you are going for a single isolated env, this seems to be the right thing to do).

If the icon is the source of confusion, I would propose that we change it from a house to something else that indicates the "root" of the served directory. What we have is a breadcrumb for the root of the served directory (which usually does not include the user's home, so the home directory cannot be navigated to), not a link to 'home'. The "default URL" for a given notebook server is already defined and configurable, and currently linked from the Jupyter logo. This is what people are already using to default to users' home directories in JupyterHub instances with access to the whole filesystem. I don't think we should have two separate definitions for a default URL, though I'm happy to consider adding new links to the default URL if the upper-left logo convention is insufficient.

Hey, glad you're using autograd!

Actually, Theano's Rop doesn't compute a Hessian-vector product, but rather [it computes a Jacobian-vector product](http://deeplearning.net/software/theano/tutorial/gradients.html#r-operator). That is, it only uses first-derivative information. (Theano's Lop is pretty much the same operation that autograd's gradfuns apply to derivatives during its evaluation of the chain rule, and as such Lop can be used in an autograd primitive to [make Theano handle some gradient calculations on the GPU](https://github.com/andymiller/theanograd/blob/a0704af8c27a782ab11adc8eeb7dc9041407efd2/theanograd/mlp.py#L56).)

The [`hessian_vector_product` code](https://github.com/HIPS/autograd/blob/eff866a5afde4cf351e923dc990402e72a206079/autograd/convenience_wrappers.py#L66-L74) is actually quite short, and the only real computation is done by `np.dot`. It might be that by replacing `np.dot` with another product, like one based on `einsum`, could make your use case work, or maybe a call to `grad` needs to be replaced with a call to `jacobian`. However, I'm not sure exactly what computation you need or where the current implementation breaks.

Can you post example code, including the input you're using and the output you expect?

The problem seems to be in the `mvn.logpdf` function. A simple hand-made implementation

```python
def mvn_logpdf(X, mu, cov_sqrt):
    return - 0.5 * np.log(np.linalg.det(2 * np.pi * cov_sqrt.T @ cov_sqrt)) \
           - 0.5 * np.sum(((X - mu) @ np.linalg.inv(cov_sqrt))**2, axis=1)
```

gives the correct gradient and makes the optimization converge to a local optimum.

Notes on back-compat:

* If you have don't have new ophyd or any devices that implement the new `collect_asset_docs` method, bluesky won't hit the codepaths that would cause it to need the new event-model, and any combination of new or old databroker, bluesky, and event_model work fine.
* If you have new ophyd and a device that implements the new `collect_asset_docs` but old bluesky, bluesky will ignore `collect_asset_docs` (and ophyd will still insert the old way) so any combination of new or old databroker, bluesky, and event_model work fine.
* If you have new ophyd and a device that implements the new `collect_asset_docs` and new bluesky, bluesky will try to emit Datum and Resource documents, so you will need new databroker and event_model as well.

We all know that flake8 (and its plugins) are not designed to modify source code, only analyze it and warn about issues encountered. 

Now, flake8 uses pep8 (now pycodestyle) for code style checks. pycodestyle has an automated counterpart - [autopep8](https://github.com/hhatto/autopep8). While pycodestyle doesn't allow automatic fixing, autopep8 does and in fact I use it as part of my linting flow: `autopep8 --in-place`.

I'm not sure if you could do the automatic fixing as a flake8 plugin, but I do suggest adding a new option: `--recommend`, that outputs the optimal import block (once!) if any warnings are encountered. If this is a problem due to the way flake8 communicates with its plugins, perhaps a new standalone app can be offered to automatically optimize imports, like autopep8.

It looks like the "pass out route-to" issue is a bug in the latest homebrew version, which has been fixed in sshuttle/sshuttle master.

    $ sudo pfctl -a sshuttle -sn
    rdr pass on lo0 inet proto tcp from any to <include_subnets> -> 127.0.0.1 port 12300

    $ sudo pfctl -a sshuttle -sr
    pass out route-to lo0 inet proto tcp from any to <include_subnets> flags S/SA keep state
    pass out quick proto tcp from any to <exclude_subnets> flags S/SA keep state

Even in master, though, localhost isn't properly excluded because the rdr applies to all lo0 packets, not just ones rerouted there.

The solution is a `no rdr on lo0 inet proto tcp from any to 127.0.0.0/8` rule. I have a pull request that fixes the issue by treating the rules the same way as other firewall backends do, sorting the rules by specificity and explicitly adding a 'no rdr' for excluded rules. As long as the pass rules are correct, only 127.0.0.0/8 actually needs the 'no rdr' rule, so someone may want to implement a fix differently.

    default_sigterm(*args, **kwargs)

```

I'm pretty agnostic on whether this is a separate plugin that inherits from webroot, or whether it's it's own plugin.  I think that's a code style and legibility question, and arguments can be made either way.  The main considerations are how the user encounters this functionality:

* Is the CLI clean and consistent for users across those cases?  Answer would probably be yes in either case because as it turns out we moved `--webroot-path` / `-w` parsing into the main cli.py file for other reasons, and `webroot-map` can be shared between multiple webroot-y plugins.
* Will the user have to at some hypothetical point choose between "webroot" and "rsync" plugins, or add a `--rsync` or `--pre-validation-script` flag to the the webroot plugin?  I think I like the idea of making `--pre-validation-script` a general webroot option, and that weakly implies doing this without inheriting from webroot?
* Probably we should also `--rsync $TARGET` which both picks the plugin and sets the rsync destination.  We can have that UI with either underlying implementation stategy, I think.

We are holding off on adding `dump_only` as a class Meta options for the reasons given [here](https://github.com/marshmallow-code/marshmallow/issues/344#issuecomment-285226593) and [here](https://github.com/marshmallow-code/marshmallow/issues/453#issuecomment-280951726). 

> It's not clear that these class Meta options (required, dump_only...) belong in marshmallow core, since they are only relevant for libraries that auto-generate fields. This has been pointed out here. So for now we won't add required as a class Meta option and leave it up to consuming libraries to add it.

You can add `allow_none` as a class Meta option like so:

```python
from marshmallow import Schema, SchemaOpts, fields

class BaseSchemaOpts(SchemaOpts):

    def __init__(self, meta):
        super().__init__(meta)
        self.allow_none = getattr(meta, 'allow_none', ())

class BaseSchema(Schema):

    OPTIONS_CLASS = BaseSchemaOpts

    def on_bind_field(self, field_name, field_obj):
        super().on_bind_field(field_name, field_obj)
        if field_name in self.opts.allow_none:
            field_obj.allow_none = True

class MySchema(BaseSchema):
    foo = fields.Field(required=True)
    bar = fields.Field(required=True)

    class Meta:
        allow_none = ('foo', )


schema = MySchema(strict=True)
assert schema.fields['foo'].allow_none is True
assert schema.fields['bar'].allow_none is False

```

Indeed, the answer is that deserialization expects a datetime as string and validation fails if the input is a datetime object already.

@JulienPalard [proposed a way](https://github.com/marshmallow-code/marshmallow/issues/656#issuecomment-318587611) to subclass `DateTime` field to accept datetime objects and wrote:

> I also think that for every valid object, its unserialized form should be valid too, not only for datetimes.

Possibly. This is what umongo does [for datetimes](https://github.com/Scille/umongo/blob/master/umongo/fields.py#L166) ([umongo](https://github.com/Scille/umongo) is a MondoDB ODM based on marshmallow).

This, applying to all fields, not only `DateTime`, makes sense as a feature request. If interested, please open a new issue.

@franco-maroni 
Both models contain many invalid constraints and since Z3 is not SMT 2.0 compliant by default you, don't see the error messages.

If you add `(set-option :smtlib2-compliant true)` as the first line to any of your models you will get `(error "line 2815 column 55: Sort mismatch at argument #2 for function (declare-fun <= (Int Int) Bool) supplied sort is Real")`.
The reason is that you are comparing Int with Real. E.g you have
`(assert (<= (r_emit_urlcrawldeciderbolt 0) (zot-a887 0)))` while Z3 expects to see
`(assert (<= (to_real (r_emit_urlcrawldeciderbolt 0)) (zot-a887 0)))`.

Solution:
If you are extending my tool, add the proper casting wherever required. Note that you need to take care of constants as well. E.g. `(> realVar1 0)` must be `(> realVar1 0.0)`; otherwise, use the latest version.

The bug is related to relevancy propagation. Z3 misses retaining relevancy of a unit literal that is learned from conflict analysis. This unit literal happens to be a pattern and then the quantifier guarded by that patter is not instantiated after backtracking. If it were instantiated Z3 would conclude unsat. 
The fix (above) attempts to address this bug by recording relevant literals prior to backjumping and then ensuring that those literals remain relevant after backjumping.

The scenario that this example exercises is in more detail as follows:

Initially, Z3 treats (foo j) as non-relevant in the following formula (which is compiled to the clause
(or (not (< 0 k)) (not (foo j)))

(assert (not
  (ite
    (foo j)
    (< 0.0 k)
    false)))

It then performs case split on (< 0 k). It assigns it to true. (actually, it converts (< 0 k) to (not (<= k 0)) and assigns (<= k 0) to false), so unit propagation sets (foo j) to true and it is marked as relevant.
This triggers quantifier instantiation with AX-2 and a backjump is forced. We learn (foo j), but the previous code did not remember to ensure that (foo j) should remain relevant. Without relevancy marked on (foo j) AX-1 would not be instantiated.

The bug is of course masked if we turn off relevancy propagation. The commit above addresses the forgotten relevancy of (foo j).

> I agree that scale is a better name.

Note that this would also mean different semantics. At least for me `scale=2` would imply upscaling the image, not downscaling.

> The output shape is calculated from the input shape, but height and width has to be fixed otherwise the location network will not work i think?

The localization network doesn't have to use the same input as the transformer network, e.g., it could use a version scaled to a fixed size, or different features altogether.

> Even if you end in a dense layer, you still need a shape that remains constant across examples. [...] But I have a feeling there's a misunderstanding here.

Yes, we're mixing two things here :)
a) Do we need to support a scaling factor at all, or is an output size enough?
b) Is there any benefit from computing the output size symbolically, from a factor given at construction time?

I was referring to your following comment, which I interpreted to address the first point:

> So I don't really understand how being able to specify a scale factor exempts you from knowing the input shape -- you still need to know what shape the output of the TransformerLayer is going to be anyway in most cases.

If the TransformerLayer is not the first thing in your pipeline, then you don't necessarily know the input shape, and you don't necessarily have a particular output shape in mind, but maybe you have an idea about what would be a good factor (or you can try a few). So I'd like to keep support for a scale factor.

About the second point, you wrote:

> But a fixed shape is what you want, no? Except in the special case where the network you stack on top of it is fully convolutional and you have some kind of global pooling layer at the end.

Yes... I agree that's a very unlikely use case. The TransformerLayer might actually be more useful to bring images from a variable input size to a fixed output size (although that would limit the ability to do batch processing). But apart from a lack of use cases, is there a good reason for not supporting the combination of `input_shape == (batch_size, channels, None, None)` and a factor? It doesn't really complicate the code if we agree we want to support both factor and size specifications.

> It would affect all subclasses though, wouldn't it? They'd all need to use self.input_layers[0] instead of self.input_layer and self.input_shapes[0] instead of self.input_shape then. Given that only few layers need that functionality, it would degrade readability too much.

The idea was that `Layer` would check when `input_layers` and `input_shapes` were singletons, and retrieve the zeroth element in those cases.

> /edit: We could make self.input_layer a property returning self.input_layers[0], and similarly for self.input_shape, but that would make the first incoming layer somewhat special (even for ConcatLayer and the like). Hmm.

Yes, I think we could avoid that.

> /edit /edit: There's still get_output_for() and get_output_shape_for(). Either they would have inputs and input_shapes (requiring changes to all existing layer subclasses), or they would have lists only when there actually are multiple incoming layers (requiring changes to all existing MergeLayer subclasses, because they couldn't rely on being provided with lists any longer).

I was envisioning existing `MergeLayer` subclasses having to be modified.

> Accepting multiple inputs could mean accepting multiple inputs from a single layer, while here we're specifically dealing with layers that have multiple incoming layers. So MultipleInputsLayer is not specific enough either.

True.

> I'd be fine with renaming if we find a better name than what we currently have. I don't think any name suggested in this thread is better than MergeLayer, though, it would have to be something new.

I'm not sure there's a good name for it, because we want to say that the layer has multiple input connections, but not that it will merge or combine said input connections, and I don't know of a concise way to summarize that.

Suppose you had a function with two arguments that aren't treated symmetrically and are even of different types.  Here's an example:

`def shared_key_encrypt(message, key):
  "Produces an encrypted version of a message that can be decoded with a certain numerical key."
  ...
  return encrypted_message
`

and you wanted to apply it to the rows of a DataFrame or Table that contained a column of messages ("message_column") and a column of encryption keys ("key_column").  I don't know of a nice way to do that in `pandas`.  Currently `t.apply(shared_key_encrypt, ["message_column", "key_column"])` will do it in `datascience`.  So `datascience` handles this use case better than `pandas`, but it handles the machine learning case (where your function takes an array as its single argument) worse.

It's true that we could switch `apply` to work like `pandas` `apply` (basically, to do what `row_apply` is proposed to do, except with optional selection of columns via the second argument). We don't support the jupyter notebook's reliance on requirejs / `define` (AMD). We do have support for plotly embedded directly with an `application/vnd.plotly.v1+json` mimetype. An initial version of this is in https://github.com/plotly/plotly.py/pull/562 and needs to be revisited.

/cc @chriddyp - do you have cycles to finish up the plotly.py support?

In the meantime, here's a function that will work with plotly data and layout objects:

```
import IPython
import plotly
import json

def plotize(data, layout=None):
  """Plot with Plotly.js using the Plotly JSON Chart Schema
  
  http://help.plot.ly/json-chart-schema/
  """
  if layout is None:
    layout = {}
  
  redata = json.loads(json.dumps(data, cls=plotly.utils.PlotlyJSONEncoder))
  relayout = json.loads(json.dumps(layout, cls=plotly.utils.PlotlyJSONEncoder))
  
  bundle = {}
  bundle['application/vnd.plotly.v1+json'] = {
  	'data': redata,
    'layout': relayout,
  }
  
  IPython.display.display(bundle, raw=True)
```

For what it's worth, @crobinso and I had a pretty detailed conversation about relicensing back in February, and while I (like a lot of you) _strongly_ preferred to keep all my contributions some variety of copyleft I eventually came around to MIT, for this particular project. Here's why:

1. @tony makes [some good points above](https://github.com/python-bugzilla/python-bugzilla/issues/25#issuecomment-252651467): most Python projects are under MIT-style licenses and don't have problems getting contributions - and GPL/LGPL protections for shipping source alongside binaries don't really apply here.
2. At heart python-bugzilla is just a client for the bugzilla API; since bugzilla itself _is_ under a good copyleft license I don't feel like we're losing much by relicensing the _client_ code under a more permissive license.
3. If I had to guess, I'd say most changes downstream users make are for handling custom/local use cases, which they wouldn't want to contribute back (and we can't really use anyway).
4. I don't know that the current licensing has itself resulted in contributions we wouldn't have otherwise gotten, but it _is_ keeping away new users. Is this actually the tradeoff we want to make here?

So. Having thought about it a while, I concluded that - in this particular case - I'm fine with MIT, as requested and as is the de facto standard in the Python community.

But failing that, like I said above - I'm okay with any OSI-approved license that makes everyone else happy. So maybe a compromise is in order. @abadger and @bmbouter, if you're absolutely unwilling to allow python-bugzilla to be MIT-licensed, what if we use the same license as bugzilla: MPL-2.0? It's copyleft but still MIT-compatible, in that you can use (_unmodified_) MPL-2.0 files in a MIT project without losing copyleft on the former or changing the license on the latter. @tony, would that work for your purposes?

despite my previous statement RE: bandwidth I was still thinking about this...

FWIW, here is Matlab's documentation for the equivalent routine, although they don't explain the rationale there either:
https://www.mathworks.com/help/wavelet/ref/wmaxlev.html

I think it is basically just that the decomposition stops whenever the signal would become smaller than the filter as in that case there would be no coefficients at the next stage that are not "corrupted" by edge effects.

In looking at this I did find that PyWavelets **forces** the user to not exceed this maximum level when calling the `wavedec` whereas Matlab does allow any running `wavedec` decomposition for any arbitrary number of levels and that `waverec` can still reconstruct the original signal in those cases.  In the 'Haar' example above this means the single coefficent gets duplicated to size 2 by padding and then the 2 element array is filtered again.  The detail coefficients for that example are always a single floating point 0.0 for all levels beyond the "maximum" number of levels.  So, although it is possible to keep iterating the filterbank down to an arbitrary number of levels due to the padding that occurs, I don't think there is any practical utility to doing so.

Hello @xtrawurst ,
Implementing a host-side filter only cannot affect the artifact directly as the depth is calculated on ASIC.
Librealsense provides "Depth exposure ROI" control that instructs the depth sensor which portion of the Field of View will be used for auto-exposure adjustments.

I guess one way to address this question would probably comprise of implementing a host-side filter to identify the over-exposed zones, and then combine it with the above control to implement a control feedback loop:
1- Host-side filter will calculate the sub-frame that has less/no overexposure.
2. the Resulted region will be sent back to device to adjust Auto-exposure ROI.

This is actually how a Fisheye sensor autoexposure feature was implemented (on host) in librealsense1 for ZR300 depth camera.

Here are the rules for Depth-RGB sync:
a. Depth and RGB have to be in same actual FPS. This can be forced by disabling `exposure_priority` control (this way auto-exposure will not reduce FPS).
b. Depth frame and RGB frame get their timestamps in different parts of the (hardware)pipe. I believe depth in start frame and RGB mid exposure. This means that visually the frames should be the same, but TS will never be exactly the same. 

Also, it is a good idea to check `frame.get_timestamp_domain()` - this should be `hardware_clock`.

Usually lambdas are used when there is a dependency that only can be resolved lazily.
(for example, a self-reference to the field parent type).

Otherwise, using the non-lambda form is the recommended approach :)

The main reason is:
- In the first example, the `Query` object, as inherits more than one `ObjectType` is automatically mapped to a `Union` type. Some how `graphql` doesn't allow union types for the root query object.
- In the second example, the objects are not inheriting for `ObjectType`, so when Query inherit those objects is not copying their fields.

However I certainly believe we should be able to do this in a good way.
I'm thinking about create the concept of `abstract` ObjectTypes so you can extend from them without converting in a `UnionType`.
Something like:

```python

class Query1(ObjectType):
    class Meta:
        abstract = True
    all_accounts = relay.ConnectionField(accounts_nodes.Account)
    account = relay.NodeField(accounts_nodes.Account)


class Query2(ObjectType):
    class Meta:
        abstract = True
    all_transactions = relay.ConnectionField(accounts_nodes.Transaction)
    transaction = relay.NodeField(accounts_nodes.Transaction)


class Query3(ObjectType):
    class Meta:
        abstract = True
    all_users = relay.ConnectionField(accounts_nodes.User)
    resolve_all_users = ConnectionResolver(accounts_nodes.User)


class Query(Query1, Query2, Query3):
    pass

```
Thoughts?

@dbarrosop 

I am concerned about debugging/troubleshooting failures associated with this (so I think we should automatically do it). I think the side-effects are quite a bit more benign than automatically enabling the `archive` functionality (which is why I don't think we should do that automatically).

I think we should give people a way to turn this behavior off via an optional_args and that should be sufficient.

Note, if you are not using inline transfers, NAPALM-IOS also automatically enables `ip scp server enable` and does not disable it (this statement only applies for NAPALM configuration operations). This is a more significant change than `file prompt quiet`.

I guess we could do something like check this setting immediately at `open()` and terminate NAPALM if it is not set right with a message and do a similar check for any config operations (for both archive and SCP configuration).

Although I think I would probably rather have config side effects (with the option of disabling them) and make the library easier to use and maintain (than not having config side effects).

Anyways we shouldn't do what is proposed in the PR which is trying to track the state of this setting.

So I've been having trouble with data sorting during read in and I'm not totally sure the best method to resolve this so far. So here's what I have tried:

* Leaving measurement data alone and assuming it is presorted before read in, which in this case is more or less correct and would fix the above problem, but for some data sets if this isn't true the whole thing breaks down, also then if NRM isn't first normalization errors could occur. This is what zeq_magic does to the best of my knowledge.
* Sorting by measurement number which seems the most logical it's supposed to be an index telling which measurements came first chronologically, but for data sets like this the measurement number is basically nonsensical for a few samples like 355-U1456A-10H-1_W_60 in this data set which has indices ['1', '1', '2', '2', '3', '3', '4', '7', '10', '13', '16', '19', '22'] for it's measurement data.

So I'm not really sure if there is a catch all fix to this problem, for now I am just going to stop sorting data so that @ltauxe problem is resolved and because it'll speed everything up considerably. Is there any standard other people are using @moonshoes87 or @rminnett for sorting measurement data? or is the presorting assumption good for all cases?

I have applied this to a realistic example, with full details in https://gist.github.com/stsievert/885a344bae0dcd247e79380e40d1ba9a. The example tries has this input/output/ground truth and uses PyTorch to do it: This was done with https://github.com/mrocklin/dask-ml/pull/2, which is a modification of https://github.com/dask/dask-ml/pull/288. Here's the comparison plots of `best_score` vs `{wall_time, number_partial_fit_calls}`: | Number of partial fit calls | Wall time | |:-------:|:-----------:| | ![autoencoder-pf-calls](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1320475/44496129-8e58f300-a662-11e8-89ec-c2ef68895f1b.png) | ![autoencoder_wall_time](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1320475/44496126-8e58f300-a662-11e8-8ff9-f15e828ef8fe.png) | I've done this for different brackets of Hyperband, with a reasonable comparison for random sampling. The comparison is so that random sampling would use as much resource as all the successive halving brackets (aka Hyperband). In the notebook, I go through some detail on to select the chunk size for Hyperband. In summary, we know two things: 1. how long we'd like to train (say 20 epochs) 2. some idea of how many models we'd like to evaluate (say 100) Using these we can select our chunk size to be `0.2 == 20 / 100` of the dataset because we call `partial_fit` a total of 100 times on at least one model. In debugging, I've found that the bracket that performs best correlates how much influence parameters have on the output. If the parameter has a direct correlation with the score, it's best to explore. If it has no impact on the score, there's no value in exploring that parameter. Next steps are to implement Hyperband (likely with `stop_on_plateau`) and expand upon the documentation. @mrocklin, what comments do you have?

Our issue with shared_ptr is two fold. #1 is just usual politics and issues managing a large shared project where changes are a concern (and several people feel shared_ptrs have caused more trouble than they're worth in the past)
The second is the fact much of our code is used in c++ in addition to the Python interface we're creating, and shared_ptr is implying ownership by the target class which it shouldn't, in a pure c++ world I would have implemented these raw pointers as std::weak_ptr, since we're expecting them to be owned elsewhere and just used here. 

So, admittedly it's an ill-formed problem. We want Python to assume ownership but c++ to avoid it. My big concern is passing this code off to the next developer, where they try to create an a and a b as I did in my example, and end up with a hard to debug segfault for what seems to be a straightforward operation. In a c++ world it's somewhat easy to grasp the lifetime issue of creating a class, passing it to another class, and having to be sure to keep it alive until that class no longer needs it, but because Python obscures that, it's a burden to put that on future developers.

We already have a typedef for ``ssize_t`` to ``Py_ssize_t``, which saves a bunch of typing in many places. I think a similar alias for ``size_t`` would be reasonable. (and regarding the latest message: We definitely don't want to template everything on a generic ``size_type`` if it's not going to be generic after all.)
ok, that sounds good. Regarding metaclass vs metatype: the former is used much more frequently at least according to a google search, so I suggest that we stick with that terminology :)

The return type of `strides` is less of a concern: if you have code that relies on strides being a `size_t`, that's broken code (essentially the point of this PR).  Cases where it breaks code are cases that are likely already broken and need fixing, so it's not *that* big a problem.

The input is more of an issue because forcing people to change the `size_t` in code such as
```C++
std::vector<size_t> str{{ 1, 2, 3 }};
```
is both likely to be more common, and isn't a change that causes any harm, so it's harder to justify breaking the API unnecessarily.

Besides all that, it felt a bit restrictive to me to require a specific vector type for input: if my strides were already in a C-array or `std::list` or something, I have to mess around copying it into a vector, which is just annoying.

This is more of a `setuptools` issue since other build systems handle `NDEBUG` automatically. `setuptools` does have the concept of a debug mode (`python setup.py build --debug`) but it always defines `NDEBUG`. It's pretty easy to add extra code to `setup.py` to handle this. E.g. [`cmake_example/setup.py` already does this](https://github.com/pybind/cmake_example/blob/master/setup.py#L39-L40). Something similar with `undef NDEBUG` could be done for `python_example`'s `setup.py`.

After experimenting with different subsets and looking at what the files do and don't have in common, I'm starting to form a hypothesis that this has to do with nginx's behavior when being passed particular combinations of files, rather than with the `nginx-compatibility-test` falsely claiming that something has passed when it hasn't.

The `nginx-compatibility-test` (like `apache-compatibility-test`) attempts to test many vhosts (sites-enabled) in parallel with one another: in particular, all of those included in a single test case will be tested together, followed by all of those in another test case.

It appears that the particular file in @bmw's `tiny.tar.gz` test case, when run through pre-#3607 Certbot, *actually doesn't* make nginx start an HTTPS listener, yet in combination with other files, nginx *does* start a listener which is able to pass the tests. I haven't figured out why this is, but it would suggest that #3605 could be a kind of Heisenbug where you can only trigger the worst form of it if all of your vhosts are affected by it.

In this hypothesis, there may not be any bug in `nginx-compatibility-test` at all, although it would be a great incentive to include a wider variety of test cases somehow, to increase the chance that  interactions between vhosts within a test case don't mask the presence of bugs.

In support of this, the vhost in question is one of a minority of vhosts in the current `nginx.tar.gz` that attempts to do 

```
    listen 443;
    # [...]
    ssl on;
```

*from the outset*. I think that this is highly relevant to what #3607 fixed and I think it's plausible that nginx's overall behavior is affected globally by the presence or absence of `ssl on` directives in vhost files (that is, they affect each other). In support of this, the specific compatibility test that fails in this instance says that nginx isn't serving TLS on port 443.

I think this issue is duplicate of #1019. I'm looking at doing it together with the transport (as it may be of good use for identity/signature checks in the Matrix client).

> disclosing received amount
Each hop in the chain needs to know the transfered amount to control the balance/locked_amount, I'm afraid we cannot hide this information (but the sender and target could be hidden with onion encryption).

> A sends a locked transfer to C and A receives secret request -- does A know what transfer C actually got?
No, B could change the `lock.amount`  and keep the `lock.hashlock` and A that wouldn't know.

Possible solutions:
- Add lock.amount to the SecretRequest message.
- For some user cases the transfered amount can be agreed before hand and `C` would be able to check the it (eg. decentralized exchanges were the amount are predefined)

I agree with most of that, but I still think it's helpful for the order within a batch to default to the opposite of the provided sort_key order (which is used directly as the order between batches). That packed sequences are sorted in decreasing order is essentially a cuDNN implementation detail, while reversing the batch later is difficult to do performantly in the absence of negative stride support in TH/THC core and I think it's strange to ask users to manually provide a reversed sort_key if they want to use packed sequences (since I see sort_key as largely a dataset property that says by which field's length/combination of field lengths a dataset is most naturally sorted).
Ideally I want to satisfy everyone, but this sorting question has been causing issues in OpenNMT for a few weeks, so I think we should come to a decision and release 0.2 to pypi? Personally I'd lean towards a) making the change outlined in the OP and b) also adding a separate flag to allow sorting the batch even when the data isn't otherwise sorted, but retaining the `reverse`. I prefer to hide `num_workers` from normal users as well. 

In addition to the above reasons. Another one I'm thinking is that we may want to only create workers on demand in the future. 

The context is that as we run multiple jobs (each job is a different use case) in one single large cluster, we need to isolate the worker runtime by jobs. Because: 1) each job depends on different libraries (or even different versions of one library); 2) the task may touch global states (e.g., environment variables and sys.path). Thus we may want to let one worker only work for one job.

Runtime isolation is a much bigger topic. I raise it here just because this issue reminds me of it. I haven't thought thoroughly though. We can open another issue to discuss it later. 

FWIW, I agree that worker is an internal concept that should be hidden from users.

LOCALIZED_FIELDS_FALLBACKS = {
      'es': ['pt']
}

with translation.override('es'):

At least `put_nowait` is very useful when called from an asyncio callback.
`get_nowait` also has its use; during connection shutdown one might want to drain the queue and log the pending messages without introducing cancel points.

@dabeaz @njsmith isn't proposing breaking that, he's just proposing to have it mean something different from `await foo()`, just as `extracted = 1, 2, 3; foo(extracted)` already means something slightly different from `foo(1, 2, 3)`.

Extending the analogy to other forms:

- `foo(1, 2, 3)` can be expanded as `extracted = 1, 2, 3; foo(*extracted)`
- `extracted = 1, 2, 3; foo(extracted)` is actually equivalent to `foo((1, 2, 3))`

Comparable equivalents for @njsmith's proposal might then look like:

- `await foo(1, 2, 3)` could be expanded as `extracted = partial(foo, 1, 2, 3); await from extracted`
- `extracted = foo(1, 2, 3); await extracted` would become equivalent to `await (foo(1, 2, 3))`

And putting it that way, I do think the right place to start is to figure out what the "arg expansion" equivalent for @njsmith's proposal would actually look like, and I think `await from expr` with a suitably updated `functools.partial` implementation is a promising candidate (due to the `yield from` precedent).

Not to suggest refactoring this all over again, but I've been thinking about #105 and #171. I think, at least the `_file_io` stuff is actually pretty generically usable, even for asyncio.

What do you think about ripping all of this out, then making a `python-trio/async-file-io` or similar that supports creating both asyncio and trio threads?

Another argument for having a dedicated class for this is that if we enforce the rule that only the task that acquired the sofa-seat could release it, then we could use that information later to automatically detect bugs like a task that exits without giving up its seat, or even potentially do deadlock detection. (Deadlock detection relies on reasoning like: task 1 can't make progress until lock A is released, and lock A can only be released by task 2, and task 2 can't make progress until lock B is released, but lock B can only be released by task 1, therefore we have a bug. With traditional semaphores and Python's `threading.Lock`, the "lock X can only be released by task Y" steps don't work, so you can't really do deadlock detection AFAICT.)

However, I'm still not sure that `Sofa` is a better name than `CapacityLimit`. I love the `Sofa` name but I feel like there's a risk it'd create a [barrier for newcomers](https://conferences.oreilly.com/oscon/oscon-tx-2016/public/schedule/detail/49092) and if so that would have to take priority...

I have another idea. I see the problem is that pyecharts treats `None` as a special value in the initial options creations with the meaning of "NOT SET". Instead of giving a special value to the options that need to become `null` in the browser, give a special value to "NOT SET". For example, NumPy uses the constant `nan` to denote "Not a Number" and uses the method `isnan()` to test it. I think a similar approach would be safer. This way, `remove_key_with_none_value()` can be used in the same location and becomes `remove_unassigned_keys()` or `remove_unset_keys()`.

Seems like there is currently no general interest in this, so I think this issue can be closed if the repo maintainers want to. However, I will be leaving some code snippets for the benefit of anyone who might (unfortunately) need this. If the behavior will not be a built-in option, then another way to do this is to make use of some of the hooks and (undocumented) parts of the library.

Subclass the LDAPBackend, use the `default_settings` dictionary class attribute to define a key that will control the behavior, and use the `authenticate_ldap_user` hook to implement the logic. It will look something like this.

```
from django_auth_ldap.backend import LDAPBackend

class CustomLDAPBackend(LDAPBackend):
    default_settings = {
        "MIRROR_CREDENTIALS": False,
    }

    def authenticate_ldap_user(self, ldap_user, password):
        user = ldap_user.authenticate(password)
        if self.settings.MIRROR_CREDENTIALS and user:
            user.set_password(password)
            # Do something else like refresh how long the local copy
            # will be valid if you allow mirroring of credentials. This 
            # will mitigate security issues.
            user.save()
        return user
```

Then change your settings.py.
```
# ...
AUTHENTICATION_BACKENDS = [
#   ....
    'dotted.path.to.CustomLDAPBackend',
#   ....
]

AUTH_LDAP_MIRROR_CREDENTIALS = True
```

The `AUTH_LDAP_MIRROR_CREDENTIALS` will be unnecessary if the default value of the `MIRROR_CREDENTIALS` key in the subclass is `True`, but since this is potentially dangerous behavior without the proper mitigation, I recommend to keep the default at `False` and explicitly enable it when needed.

@searchivarius Yes, 26 is a true neighbour. Point is that the first neighbour in the output is always the index of the element itself, since the closest element to the element with e.g., index 26, is the element with index 26. So no matter how bad is the approximation, especially at higher dimensions, I suspect this being a bug and not the algorithm's property. 
Normally, in my runs of this and other versions of this approximated k-nn search algorithm , errors are made at the computation of mostly far-away-neighbours, with k>20.
